name: 麺 みつヰ (Men Mitsui)

noodles.notes: Hand-kneaded (te-momi) to order in small batches, giving irregular crinkles that grip the soup; springy, chewy bite, boiled just firm. A thin straight noodle option is also offered alongside the signature medium-thick hand-kneaded noodle.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shoyu ramen with hand-kneaded medium-thick noodles, topped with ajitama (seasoned egg)
ramen.chashu: Two styles served together: low-temperature-cooked pork loin with a delicate, ham-like texture, and charcoal-grilled (aburi) pork belly for sweet rendered fat and a smoky char aroma.

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-based and the shoyu tare relies on soy sauce; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on whole chicken stock (with seafood/dried-goods depth) and the shop's signature toppings include pork chashu; no plant-based broth is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A quietly refined Tokyo chicken-shoyu bowl: whole-chicken stock is coaxed into a glassy, semi-transparent amber broth, then finished with a pool of chicken fat and a tare blended from three different soy sauces for layered, restrained umami rather than salt or heft. It sits in the modern Tokyo shoyu lineage — the kind of clear, delicate, technique-driven bowl built to let hand-kneaded wheat noodles and a whole-chicken stock speak for themselves.

name: 中華ソバ ビリケン (Chuka Soba Biliken)

ramen.chashu: Duck breast chashu, seared/grilled (aburi-style) first to render off excess fat before slicing — lean, juicy, and distinct from the usual pork loin/belly.

gf.note: No gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented; the hand-kneaded wheat noodles and shoyu-based tare are standard.

noodles.notes: Hand-kneaded (te-momi) in-house from Hokkaido wheat; thick, curly/crimped strands with a chewy, springy bite.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Tokyo craft-soba bowl built on a five-hour whole-chicken and duck stock layered with a water-extracted niboshi-kombu dashi — the duck's musky richness is tamed by chicken into a clean, additive-free umami rarely found in classic shoyu ramen. It reads as an update of old-school Tokyo chuka soba, swapping pork for poultry and dried-fish depth.
ramen.signature_bowl: 手もみ中華ソバ (Hand-Kneaded Chinese Soba) — the shop's award-recognized bowl, shoyu-tare duck-and-chicken broth with hand-kneaded noodles and duck chashu.

vegan.note: No plant-based broth or chashu-free vegan option is documented; the broth is duck/chicken/niboshi-based throughout.

name: 浅草来々軒 (Asakusa Rairaiken)

noodles.notes: Made from 'Sato no Sora' wheat bred to carry genetics traced back to Meiji-era wheat strains, in an effort to reproduce the 1910 original; described as elastic and springy with a smooth, easy-slurping texture.

ramen.signature_bowl: 百年醤油らうめん (Hyakunen Shoyu Rauumen / "100-Year Soy Sauce Rauumen")
ramen.chashu: Pork chashu, seasoned then hung and roasted over direct flame in the old-fashioned way, served in generous portion as the bowl's main enrichment.
notable_toppings: slow-rehydrated menma (soaked roughly a week using an old-fashioned method before seasoning)

gf.note: Wheat-based noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare made with Yamasa soy sauce; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken stock and the bowl is topped with pork chashu and fish naruto; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is the bowl where Tokyo shoyu ramen itself began: a 1910 recipe painstakingly rebuilt from historical research into a clean, clear chicken broth laced with soy sauce, the direct ancestor of every clear-soup Tokyo shoyu ramen that followed. Nothing flashy — just old-fashioned menma, hand-roasted chashu, and a bright, savory shoyu backbone that reads as the root of the family tree rather than a modern reinterpretation of it.

name: 来集軒 (Raishūken)

ramen.chashu: Pork shoulder (kata) chashu, firmly seasoned with a dense, meaty bite rather than a soft-braised melt — sliced thick.

gf.note: Standard wheat noodles and a wheat-based shoyu tare with no gluten-free option documented anywhere.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick, curly (chijire) noodles, the classic partner for old-school Tokyo chuka soba; the shop traces back to a noodle factory, though it is not confirmed whether today's noodles are still made in-house.

notable_toppings: housemade pork wonton (in the wonton-men version)
ramen.pantheon: Raishuken pours a clear amber shoyu broth simmered slowly from chicken, pork, and vegetables — the gentle, faintly sweet chuka soba that defined Tokyo ramen for decades before tonkotsu and paitan trends arrived. Its firm-bitten pork shoulder chashu and medium curly noodles, served in a Showa-era Asakusa dining room, are a taste of the capital's original noodle-shop lineage.
ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (Chuka Soba) — the classic soy-sauce ramen; regulars also order ワンタンメン (Wonton Men), which adds housemade pork wontons to the same broth.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken and pork; no plant-based bowl is documented.

name: 竹三郎 (Takesaburo)

ramen.chashu: Classic Iekei-style braised pork chashu slices — no unusual preparation documented.

gf.note: Wheat-based Iekei noodles and a soy-based tonkotsu tare are used throughout; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

noodles.notes: Very thick, chewy straight noodles — described by diners as almost tsukemen-level thickness — with a firm, springy bite typical of Yokohama Iekei-style noodles.

ramen.pantheon: This is textbook Yokohama Iekei transplanted to Asakusa: a cloudy, deeply savory tonkotsu-shoyu broth built on a firm soy backbone, ladled over thick, chewy low-hydration noodles and dressed in the genre's signature trio of spinach, nori, and chashu. The kitchen also breaks from the family-style script with its own sizzling iron-pot tantanmen-style aburasoba, where the sesame-pork broth reduces to a thick, clinging sauce around ultra-thick noodles.
ramen.signature_bowl: 濃厚豚骨ラーメン (rich tonkotsu-shoyu ramen) — the shop's namesake bowl; the sizzling iron-pot 満州トロトロ担々汁あり油そば (tantan-style aburasoba) is the house original worth trying alongside it.

vegan.note: Pork-bone broth and pork chashu are central to this Iekei-style menu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: ニクノイロ (Niku no Iro)

ramen.chashu: Rough hand-chunked pork (not sliced thin) — irregular, generous-sized pieces of varying shape stacked into the bowl, so the meat itself becomes the main texture of the bowl rather than a garnish.

gf.note: Standard wheat ramen/tsukemen noodles from outside noodle makers and a wheat-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or menu option is documented.

noodles.notes: Diners choose thin or thick noodles at order: a straight, medium-thin noodle from Maruyama Noodle Factory, or a flat, high-hydration, springy thick noodle from Kyoto's Mennya Chigaku. A tsukemen (dipping) version of either is available for a small upcharge.

notable_toppings: kujo negi (Kyoto scallion) piled high on the kujo negi ramen
half-boiled soy-marinated egg (ajitama) on the egg ramen
ramen.pantheon: A pork-and-seafood clear soup built in the modern Tokyo shio lineage — gentle and clean on the first sip, then turning saltier and richer as chunky chashu melts its umami into the broth, lifted by a chicken-fat aroma oil. The bowl's real signature isn't a regional style but its meat-forward format: a clear soup engineered to carry an unusually large, rough-cut pile of chashu, with a nod to Kyoto in its kujo-negi option and thick noodle supplier.
ramen.signature_bowl: 名物！○得ラーメン (Meibutsu Otoku Ramen, ¥1,400) — the shop's flagship, loaded with its signature chunky chashu over a shio-based pork-seafood clear soup; choose shoyu or shio tare and thin or thick noodles.

vegan.note: Broth is pork clear soup blended with seafood stock and finished with chicken-fat aroma oil; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 浅草 熟成味噌らーめん のりあき・美々 (Asakusa Jukusei Miso Ramen Noriaki Bibi)

ramen.chashu: Two cuts served together — braised pork shoulder and pork belly, both sliced thick and simmered tender in a savory marinade.

gf.note: Wheat-based noodles and a wheat-miso tare with no rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free alternative documented.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick wavy noodles from an outside noodle maker, chosen to stand up to the rich, fat-flecked miso broth.

notable_toppings: side dish of koji-miso blended with ankimo (monkfish liver) for mid-bowl flavor change
ramen.pantheon: A New-wave miso bowl that fuses two lineages in one pot: Niigata's thick, back-fat-slicked miso richness meets Sapporo's technique of wok-roasting the miso with aromatics before it hits the pork-and-chicken stock. A custom four-miso blend, aged ten days, gives the soup a deep, almost fermented tang, and a side dish of ankimo-spiked miso lets you push the bowl even richer as you eat.
ramen.signature_bowl: Jukusei Miso Ramen (熟成味噌らーめん) — the aged four-miso blend bowl

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bone and chicken stock with pork chashu; no plant-based bowl is documented.

name: 富士らーめん (Fuji Ramen)

ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised pork belly, three tender, soft slices per bowl — no aburi char, purely simmered.

gf.note: Standard wheat ramen noodles with kansui and a soy-based tare; no rice or gluten-free noodle option is documented.

noodles.notes: In-house handmade noodles from 100% domestic wheat, medium-thin straight strands with a high-hydration, springy chew that holds up in both the ramen and the thicker tsukemen dip.

ramen.pantheon: A concentrated pork-bone broth deepened with seafood dashi and finished with a pour of black garlic oil — the mayu-on-tonkotsu combination that traces back to Kumamoto's signature style, though built here as this Asakusa counter's own take rather than a direct import. The soup reads creamy and dense on first sip but the seafood backbone keeps it from turning heavy, letting the roasted-garlic aroma carry the bowl.
ramen.signature_bowl: Fuji Tokusei Ramen (富士特製らーめん) — the full-dress bowl with ajitama, thick-sliced chashu, nori, and cabbage over the mayu-finished tonkotsu-gyokai broth.

vegan.note: Broth is pork-bone and seafood based with animal-derived tare; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 浅草名代らーめん 与ろゐ屋 (Asakusa Nadai Ramen Yoroiya)

ramen.chashu: Braised pork loin chashu, cooked so tender it falls apart at a touch of the chopsticks — mild, gently seasoned rather than punchy.

gf.note: Standard wheat-flour ramen and tsukemen noodles with a shoyu-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

noodles.notes: Curly (chijire) noodles carry the ramen; the tsukemen uses a separate medium-thick noodle milled from Nagano medium-strength flour, served cold alongside a hot wafū dipping broth with chashu, menma, scallion and mitsuba.

ramen.pantheon: Set just off Denboin-dori in Asakusa — the neighborhood often credited as the birthplace of Japan's shoyu ramen — Yoroiya keeps that old Tokyo wafū style alive: a light, clear pork-and-chicken bone broth carried by niboshi, katsuobushi and kombu, finished with a bright thread of Kochi yuzu peel and a dusting of fish powder for a deceptively deep, refined shoyu bowl.
ramen.signature_bowl: Meidai Ramen — the shop's namesake yuzu-scented wafū shoyu ramen, built on a pork-and-chicken bone broth rounded out with niboshi, katsuobushi and kombu.

vegan.note: The broth is an animal-based pork-and-chicken bone stock finished with niboshi and katsuobushi (dried fish) dashi; no plant-based bowl is documented.

name: らぁめん めん○（めんまる） (Raamen Men-maru)

noodles.notes: Soft, gently curly thin noodles built to soak up the light broth rather than fight it - an old-school Showa-era pairing.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (らぁめん), often ordered with a soy-marinated soft egg (ajitama)
ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced roast pork loin/belly, laid simply over the noodles - a modest, old-fashioned char siu rather than a thick showpiece cut.

gf.note: Wheat noodles and a wheat-based shoyu tare with no gluten-free option documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken bones and the bowl is topped with pork chashu; no plant-based broth or protein option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A time-capsule bowl of old-town Tokyo ramen: a clear, chicken-bone shoyu broth so light and clean you're tempted to drink it dry, paired with soft curly noodles in the gentle, nostalgic style that once defined countless shitamachi noodle stands. It's the taste of Asakusa's Showa-era street food, not modern craft ramen.

name: 中国手打拉麺 馬賊 浅草店（浅草本店） (Bazoku Asakusa (Chugoku Teuchi Ramen Bazoku )

noodles.notes: Hand-kneaded and hand-pulled to order at the counter (the loud slap of dough against the board is part of the show), giving noodles of slightly irregular width with a springy, almost mochi-like chew and a slick, high-hydration slipperiness.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tantanmen (担々麺) — a jet-black bowl built from pork-and-chicken broth finished with hot sesame oil, white sesame paste, chili oil and a touch of vinegar; also available as tsukemen-style dipping noodles.
ramen.chashu: Simple braised pork slices, roughly 1cm thick — a quiet supporting topping rather than the star, since the hand-pulled noodles and sesame tare carry the bowl.

gf.note: Wheat-based hand-pulled noodles and a wheat-containing shoyu/sesame tare are central to every bowl; no gluten-free noodle or broth option is documented.

vegan.note: Broths are built on pork bone and chicken, and chashu pork is a standard topping; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This Asakusa institution channels the Chinese hand-pulled la mian tradition into a Tokyo lunch-line favorite — dough is slapped, stretched and cut to order right at the counter for a springy, mochi-like bite unlike machine-cut noodles. Its calling card isn't tonkotsu but a jet-black tantanmen, where pork-and-chicken broth is married with hot sesame oil, sesame paste, chili oil and vinegar into something creamy and nutty rather than fiery.

name: 自家製麺NO11 ASAKUSA (Jikaseimen NO11 Asakusa)

ramen.chashu: Thick-cut, long-braised pork belly (nitabuta-style) — lean-leaning and so tender chopsticks slide through it. The standard bowl gets one large slab; the "Pork Ramen" variant piles on extra.

gf.note: Handmade wheat noodles made with kansui plus a shoyu-based tare — no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

noodles.notes: Self-made, extremely thick, low-hydration noodles with a flat cut and a gentle wave; texture is dense and gritty-chewy rather than springy. Standard serving is a hefty 350g (280g/180g options available).

notable_toppings: Mountain of yasai (bean sprouts and cabbage, customizable amount)
Abura (a little extra pork back-fat, free customizable topping)
ramen.pantheon: A Fujimaru-kei branch of the Tokyo Jiro family tree: pork bones and a sharp shoyu tare are simmered together into a semi-emulsified broth — neither fully clear nor fully creamy — that clings to defiantly thick, low-hydration noodles built to carry a mountain of yasai, garlic, and fat. It softens the classic Jiro gruffness just slightly with thick, long-braised pork belly that all but melts.
ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen — the shop's single core bowl (customizable with yasai, ninniku, and abura in classic Jiro call-out style), or 豚ラーメン (Pork Ramen) for a double helping of chashu.

vegan.note: Broth is pork-bone based and the signature topping is braised pork belly; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 田中そば店 浅草店 (Tanaka Sobaten Asakusa)

ramen.chashu: Pork belly (buta-bara), braised long and slow until it falls apart and melts in the mouth — not aburi-charred, just deeply tender braised belly.

gf.note: Wheat noodles and wheat-based shio/miso tare; no rice or konjac noodle substitute is documented for this shop.

noodles.notes: Flat, kinked (chijire) noodles in the Kitakata style; production source not confirmed, likely supplied rather than made in-house given the chain's scale.

notable_toppings: optional free tableside back-fat (abura) for richer flavor
optional table-side chili spice for flavor variation
ramen.pantheon: A Tokyo reading of Kitakata ramen: pork bones are simmered gently so the broth stays glass-clear rather than clouding, then seasoned with a crisp, clean shio tare instead of Kitakata's usual soy, poured over broad, wavy noodles with melt-in-the-mouth braised pork belly. This Asakusa location also keeps alive a neighborhood secret — limited-batch niboshi (dried sardine) ramen bowls carried over from the dried-fish specialist that used to occupy the same storefront.
ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (Chuka Soba) — clear pork-bone broth with crisp shio seasoning and tender braised pork belly; this location also offers rotating, limited-quantity niboshi (dried sardine) bowls such as Tsugaru Niboshi Chuka Soba and Bari Niboshi Soba not found at most other branches.

vegan.note: Broth is pork-bone (and dried-sardine for the limited niboshi bowls) with meat-based chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 浅草 生田庵（いくたあん） (Asakusa Ikutaan)

ramen.chashu: Pork shoulder (ude) and belly (bara), simmered until tender and sliced fresh to order in thick, generous cuts — juicy, fall-apart tender, and the clear centerpiece of the bowl.

gf.note: Standard wheat noodles and a straightforward shoyu tare; no gluten-free or alternative-noodle option is documented.

noodles.notes: House-produced flat, straight medium noodles with firm bite and a smooth, slippery finish, made to pair with the clear pork-chicken shoyu broth.

ramen.pantheon: Ikutaan belongs to Tokyo's newer "chan-kei" lineage of shoyu ramen: a glistening, deceptively light-looking pork-and-chicken clear broth capped with a floating layer of lard that seals in heat and animal richness beneath a clean, fragrant soy seasoning. It's crowned with an outsized mound of fresh-sliced, tender pork chashu, giving a bowl that reads assari on sight but delivers real fatty, meaty depth with every sip.
ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (Chūka Soba) — the shop's classic bowl: a shimmering clear pork-and-chicken shoyu broth topped with a generous heap of freshly sliced chashu, enough to rival a dedicated chashu-men.
sources: https://ikkos-films.com/【東京・浅草】浅草-生田庵「東京でジワジワ広が/

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bones/meat and chicken with pork chashu throughout; no plant-based broth or protein option is documented.

name: おかゆと麺のお店 粥餐庁 京王モール店（粥餐庁 新宿京王モール店） (Kayu Sanchin (Okayu to Men Kayu Sanchin) - S)

noodles.notes: Uses a whole-wheat (zenryufun) noodle chosen for extra dietary fiber and iron; exact thickness, shape and hydration are not documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: Noko Goma Tantanmen (濃厚ごま担々麺, Rich Sesame Tantanmen) — the shop's noodle bowl reviewers pair with the congee in the Half & Half set
notable_toppings: plump shrimp wontons (in the wonton-noodle version)
mushrooms (in the hot-and-sour chicken noodle version)

gf.note: Noodles are standard wheat noodles; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Menu centers on chicken-based congee stock, steamed chicken, and shrimp wontons; no plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This isn't a ramen-ya so much as a Chinese/Taiwanese congee counter that also turns out a short line of noodle bowls, rooted in a decades-old "food as medicine" (医食同源) philosophy inherited from an old-school Chinese restaurant. Its rich sesame tantanmen — built for pairing with the house congee in a half-and-half set — leans on nutty sesame depth and crunchy dried-shrimp topping rather than any single regional broth lineage, with whole-wheat noodles as the shop's health-minded twist.

name: 麺屋 鶏路地 (Menya Tori Roji)

ramen.chashu: Chicken breast chashu (not pork) — a lean, thin-sliced cut that rides on top of the chicken paitan rather than a fatty pork centerpiece; a couple of reviewers flagged it as the bowl's weak link next to the soup.

gf.note: Standard wheat ramen noodles with kansui, plus shoyu/shio tare — no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

noodles.notes: Low-hydration thin straight noodles built to carry the thick, clingy chicken paitan without going limp.

notable_toppings: ajitama (soft-boiled egg, as in the toki-tama bowl)
ramen.pantheon: Tucked into a basement stairwell off Shibuya's Center-gai, this shop pours a Kyoto-style tori-paitan — a naturally cloudy, heavily reduced chicken broth with real body and a forward, almost bone-deep chicken sweetness rather than the fattier, more perfumed style found at Tokyo's mayu-driven paitan specialists. The house "trilogy" of shoyu, shio, and tantanmen lets that same concentrated chicken stock carry three different personalities from one pot.
ramen.signature_bowl: Tori Soba (鶏そば) — the Kyoto-style chicken paitan ramen, ordered in shoyu or shio, with the toki-tama (soft egg) upgrade as the fuller version.

vegan.note: Broth is chicken paitan and chashu is chicken; no plant-based broth or protein option is documented.

name: 麺屋海神 新宿店 (Menya Kaijin Shinjuku)

gf.note: Noodles are standard wheat with kansui and the tare is a wheat-adjacent shio blend; no gluten-free noodle or menu option is documented for this shop.

noodles.notes: A custom-order thin noodle blended from two wheat flours and made with charcoal-filtered water; low-temperature aging is said to give it a firm, springy bite despite its thinness. Produced to the shop's spec by an outside noodle maker rather than hand-rolled in house.

notable_toppings: Optional heshiko (fermented grilled mackerel) onigiri to drop into the broth as a closing zosui
ramen.pantheon: This bowl runs on straight fish power: whole "ara" — the heads, bones, and frames of whatever fresh fish came in that day (tai, buri, salmon, conger eel) — are seared over flame and then patiently simmered and skimmed into a clear, golden shio broth, so the soup itself changes character day to day. It sits in Tokyo's contemporary gyokai-shio lineage rather than any old regional style, swapping out pork chashu for delicate shrimp tsumire and chicken-cartilage tsukune that echo the seafood dashi instead of fighting it.
ramen.signature_bowl: Aradaki Shio Ramen (あら炊き塩らぁめん) — the daily fresh-fish-bone salt ramen, optionally paired with a grilled heshiko onigiri to finish as a broth-soaked rice course.

vegan.note: The broth is built entirely on fish bones simmered daily, and the standard toppings (shrimp tsumire, chicken tsukune) are animal-based, so there is no plant-broth option documented.

name: 和牛らーめん 極 (Wagyu Ramen Goku)

noodles.notes: Straight, low-hydration noodles from an outside noodle maker (Kanno Seimen per one account); some reviewers describe them as flat and medium-thick with a springy, chewy bite. The mazesoba uses a similarly chewy flat noodle.

ramen.signature_bowl: 極上和牛らーめん (Gokujo Wagyu Ramen) — a milky, rich white broth simmered entirely from beef bones, finished with a shio tare, topped with a trio of wagyu preparations.
ramen.chashu: The heart of the bowl: block wagyu chashu that is low-temperature cooked to keep it pink and tender, then brushed with tare and seared/torched (aburi-style) to order; served alongside plainer beef round (momo) chashu and a separately simmered wagyu nikomi (stewed wagyu) for three distinct textures of beef in one bowl.

gf.note: No gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented; noodles are standard wheat and the tare is a conventional seasoned broth base, so this is not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The entire concept is built on a beef-bone broth and beef toppings with no documented plant-broth alternative, so there is no vegan option here.

ramen.pantheon: This bowl swaps the usual pork femurs for cattle bones, boiling them down into a milky, collagen-rich paitan that carries a cleaner, sweeter beef umami than tonkotsu, with pepper and rock salt on the table to let diners push the flavor further. A trio of wagyu — seared block chashu, plain round chashu, and simmered wagyu — turns a single bowl into a mini tasting of the animal.

name: 中華ソバ 櫻坂 (Chuka Soba Sakurazaka)

ramen.chashu: Thick-cut, melt-tender braised pork chashu — the special (tokusei) bowl piles on three generous slices.

gf.note: Standard wheat noodles and a soy-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or broth option documented.

noodles.notes: Straight, medium-thick noodles milled from pure domestic (Hokkaido) wheat; firm, chewy bite that holds up well against the thick broth and clinging fish powder.

ramen.pantheon: A Shibuya mainstay since 2004 built on Tokyo's modern double-soup style: a long-simmered pork-bone base fused with a bonito-and-niboshi seafood backbone into a shoyu broth that reads as concentrated and faintly sweet yet stays surprisingly drinkable. The same fish-pork synergy — the backbone of Tokyo's famous thick-broth tsukemen scene — shows up even more intensely in the shop's dipping noodles.
ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei Chuka Soba (特製中華ソバ) — the shoyu-tare pork-and-seafood ramen loaded with extra chashu, ajitama, and menma; the shop's tsukemen (fish-pork dipping noodles) is the other draw.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bone plus bonito/niboshi (dried sardine); no plant-based broth or vegan bowl found.

name: らーめん ぽっぽっ屋 水道橋店 (Popposhi-ya Suidobashi-ten (Ramen Popposhiya)

noodles.notes: Extra-thick, curly noodles specially commissioned from the noted Asakusa Kaikaro noodle mill, made with 100% "Ocean" brand wheat via a proprietary hand-worked process; famed for strong chew and firm bite. Not made in-house.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (らーめん) — the shop's standard, top-selling bowl: genkotsu pork-bone, chicken and vegetable broth finished with a sweet shoyu tare, ladled over the shop's signature extra-thick wavy noodles.
ramen.chashu: Chashu pork served as a hearty, thick-cut Jiro-style topping alongside the broth; specific cut and cooking method (braised vs. grilled) not documented.

gf.note: Standard wheat ramen noodles with kansui and a wheat-based shoyu tare; no gluten-free noodle or menu option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bone, chicken and animal fat (back fat is a signature free topping); no plant-based broth or vegan bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This bowl channels Tokyo's Jiro-inspired tradition — a stamina-built broth of slow-simmered pork leg bone, chicken and vegetables, rounded out with a sweet shoyu tare and topped with mountains of free bean sprouts and rendered pork back fat. It's built around famously thick, chewy, work-for-it noodles, with a rare-for-the-genre miso version and tsukemen/mazesoba spins rounding out the menu.

name: 横浜家系らーめん侍 渋谷本店 (Yokohama Iekei Ramen Samurai Shibuya Honten)

noodles.notes: Medium-thick straight noodles from Sakai Seimen (a noodle house closely associated with Iekei shops), with firm bite; diners can request noodle firmness at order time.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (らーめん) — the standard bowl, or the "zenbu-nose" all-toppings version loaded with extra chashu, ajitama, and nori
ramen.chashu: Oversized, thick-sliced braised pork (the classic iekei ōban chāshū) — tender simmered pork that drapes over the edge of the bowl.

gf.note: Wheat-based noodles and a soy-sauce tare are standard; no gluten-free noodle or broth option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on simmered pork bone and chicken with chicken-fat aroma oil, and chashu is pork; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is textbook Yokohama Iekei: a double soup of long-simmered pork bone and chicken stock, bound with a bold soy tare and finished with a glossy cap of chicken fat, in the lineage that runs back through Machidaya to the original Yoshimuraya style. It arrives with sturdy straight noodles, a thick sheet of nori, and a wide slice of braised pork big enough to hang off the bowl.

name: 道玄坂マンモス (Dogenzaka Mammoth)

ramen.chashu: Thick-cut, tender braised pork chashu chunks — offered as an optional "meat added" upgrade rather than a default component of the bowl.

gf.note: No gluten-free noodle or tare option documented; the noodles are wheat-based (including a wheat-germ blend) and the tare reads as a standard soy-based sauce.

noodles.notes: Signature is an ultra-thick, mochi-mochi wheat-germ (haiga) noodle with a nutty, slightly sweet grain flavor designed to stand up to the thick dipping broth; diners can also choose an ultra-thick plain 'mochi' noodle or a flat, wide 'birabira' noodle.

ramen.pantheon: Dogenzaka Mammoth is a direct descendant of Kichijoji's En-ji, the shop credited with pioneering "vegepota" (vegetable-potage) tsukemen — a milky pork-and-chicken paitan simmered with pureed root vegetables and a whisper of seafood dashi, giving the dipping broth a naturally sweet, velvety body you won't find in a standard tonkotsu-gyokai tsukemen. It's poured thick enough to cling to the shop's trademark ultra-thick wheat-germ noodle, built specifically to stand up to that heavy soup.
ramen.signature_bowl: Noukou Tsukemen (rich vegepota dipping noodles) with a choice of ultra-thick wheat-germ noodle — the shop's recommended pick.

vegan.note: Broth is built from pork bone, chicken bone, and seafood stock; no plant-based broth option documented.

name: 背脂ラーメン チャッチャ亭 (Seabura Ramen Chaccha-tei)

ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced pork shoulder-loin (katarosu), low-temperature cooked for a moist, tender bite rather than a heavy braise.

gf.note: Standard wheat ramen noodles and a shoyu-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or sauce swap is documented.

noodles.notes: Chewy, springy medium-thick straight noodles are standard for the ramen; some mazesoba/limited bowls appear with a curlier cut.

notable_toppings: melted pork back-fat (seabura) mounded on top
ramen.pantheon: This is Tokyo's seabura-chaccha lineage taken to an extreme: a shoyu tonkotsu broth piled with diced or melted domestic pork back-fat that you dial up from a light dusting to a bowl where the fat itself becomes half the soup. The fat doesn't just add grease — it melts into the shoyu base for a round, almost sweet richness that keeps the finish surprisingly clean rather than heavy.
ramen.signature_bowl: 背脂チャッチャラーメン — the namesake tonkotsu-shoyu ramen with customizable back-fat levels from a light dusting up to an off-menu "extreme fat" pour that turns half the bowl into rendered fat; the garlic seabura mazesoba is also a well-loved order.

vegan.note: Broth is pork-bone based and finished with pork back-fat and pork chashu; no plant-based bowl is documented.

name: ラーメン豚山 サクラステージ店 (Ramen Butayama Sakura Stage)

noodles.notes: Signature ultra-thick ("gokubuto") noodles made with no additives besides standard kansui, giving a dense, chewy bite typical of Jiro-style bowls; exact shape and hydration not documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: Small ramen (2 slices of chashu) — the classic starter order, customizable through the counter call system by adding garlic, extra vegetables, and pork back fat.
ramen.chashu: Called "kami-buta" ("god pork") — thick-cut pork slow-simmered for hours until fall-apart tender and melting, ladled generously into the bowl rather than laid as neat slices.
notable_toppings: Mountain of cabbage and bean sprouts (roughly 300g standard, lightly wilted)
garlic (not included by default — must be requested)
grated cheese and curry-flavor topping as menu add-ons, unusual for a Jiro-style shop

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-based and the tare is a soy-sauce (wheat) base; no gluten-free noodle or sauce option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built from pork bones and meat and chashu is a core component of every bowl; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: Butayama channels Tokyo's cult Jiro-kei lineage — the mountainous, garlic-and-vegetable-piled bowls that trace back to Ramen Jiro near Tokyo Institute of Technology — but softens the intensity with a pork-bone-and-meat broth that's only lightly emulsified, sitting between clear and creamy. Its calling card is a sweet-salty shoyu tare, a hefty pile of barely-wilted cabbage and bean sprouts, and slow-braised, spoon-tender pork nicknamed "kami-buta" (god pork).

name: 兆楽 道玄坂店 (Choraku Dogenzaka-ten)

noodles.notes: Thin, slightly crimped noodles with a firm bite and a pronounced yellow (kansui) hue, typical of old-school Tokyo machi-chuka ramen; not documented as handmade in-house.

ramen.signature_bowl: Roosu Ramen (ルースラーメン) — a clear chicken-bone shoyu ramen crowned with the shop's namesake "roosu": julienned pork and bamboo shoots stir-fried and bound in a glossy, oyster-sauce-accented, starch-thickened soy sauce, ladled over the noodles.
ramen.chashu: Not a chashu-slice bowl — its meat topping is the "roosu" itself: thin-sliced/julienned pork stir-fried with bamboo shoots and finished in a sweet-savory, starch-thickened soy sauce, spooned on like an ankake garnish rather than braised chashu.
notable_toppings: roosu (julienned pork & bamboo shoot stir-fry in thickened sauce)

gf.note: Wheat noodles, soy-sauce tare, and a starch-thickened oyster-sauce topping are standard throughout the menu; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is chicken-bone based and the signature topping is pork; no plant-based broth or dish is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A fixture of Tokyo's old-school machi-chuka diners since the 1950s, its base bowl is an unpretentious, clear chicken-bone shoyu ramen — the kind of chintan broth that predates the modern ramen-shop boom. The shop's real soul, though, is "roosu": julienned pork and bamboo shoots glazed in a sweet-savory, oyster-sauce-kissed, starch-thickened soy sauce and ladled over the noodles, turning the bowl into Chinese-diner comfort food more than crafted ramen.

name: 麺処 竹川 (Mendokoro Takekawa)

ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu shares the bowl with hand-formed chicken tsukune (meatballs) and tori-bushi (dried shaved chicken flakes) steeped into the dip — a chicken-forward take rather than a single pork centerpiece.

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-based (whole-grain wheat flour, custom-milled) and the dipping sauce is soy-sauce (shoyu) based; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

noodles.notes: Custom-order noodles from a specialty noodle maker, milled with whole-grain flour for a chewy, almost pasta-like bite; not made in-house but made to the shop's own spec.

ramen.pantheon: A modern, chicken-only tsukemen rather than a classical regional style: an additive-free tori-paitan dip built from branded Ibaraki chicken and simmered using the shop's night-time mizutaki (chicken hot-pot) know-how, so the soup carries real hot-pot depth instead of the usual pork-bone base. Chicken flakes, tsukune, and a rainbow of fresh Yamaguchi vegetables give the bowl a lighter, more vegetable-forward, hot-pot-adjacent character than typical Tokyo tonkotsu-gyokai tsukemen.
ramen.signature_bowl: Mutenka Tsukemen (無添加つけ麺) — additive-free chicken paitan dipping noodles

vegan.note: Broth is built entirely on chicken bones/meat and the bowl includes pork chashu and chicken tsukune; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 濃厚海老つけ麺 ともやま (Noukou Ebi Tsukemen Tomoyama)

ramen.chashu: The special bowl carries two chashu styles — tender chicken loin (torimune/rosu) and seared (aburi) pork loin — plus a shrimp wonton, rather than a single classic pork slab.

gf.note: Standard wheat-and-kansui noodles with a conventional dipping sauce; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

noodles.notes: Chewy medium-thick straight noodles (roughly 200g per regular serving), noted by diners as being on the softer side rather than super-firm.

ramen.pantheon: A shrimp-obsessed modern Tokyo tsukemen: whole shrimp heads and shells are blitzed in an in-house blender into a thick, almost bisque-like dipping soup, so the aroma and sweetness of the shrimp itself carry the bowl instead of a heavy fat slick.
ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei Noukou Ebi Tsukemen (Special Rich Shrimp Dipping Noodles), approx. ¥1,350

vegan.note: Broth is built entirely on shrimp shells/heads and the toppings include meat chashu; no plant-based bowl is documented.

name: ラーメン凪 豚王 渋谷本店 (Ramen Nagi Butao Shibuya Honten)

noodles.notes: Thin, straight Hakata-style noodles cooked quickly firm; kae-dama (noodle refill) is offered so you can order a second helping once the broth is gone.

ramen.signature_bowl: Butao (豚王) — the shop's original namesake bowl: a pork-bone broth simmered roughly 20 hours, thin straight noodles, and a mound of black-pepper-seasoned bean sprouts. Sister bowls swap in squid ink (Kuro-oh), chili (Aka-oh) or basil (Midori-oh) for variety on the same tonkotsu base.
ramen.chashu: A single piece of classic Nagi-style roast/braised pork chashu sits on top — a modest, supporting portion rather than the bowl's main event; the broth is the star here.
notable_toppings: table-side spice condiment for custom heat/flavor

gf.note: Noodles are standard wheat ramen noodles; no gluten-free noodle or tare substitute is documented for this location.

vegan.note: Broth is a long-simmered pork bone (tonkotsu) stock with pork chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented for this Shibuya location.

ramen.pantheon: Butao channels the Hakata tonkotsu tradition — thin, kae-dama-ready noodles swimming in a pork-bone broth simmered some 20 hours into a smooth, milky richness. Where many Hakata bowls lean pungent and funky, this one is coaxed toward a cleaner, mellower finish, crowned with a mound of black-pepper bean sprouts and a table-side spice packet that lets you dial the heat yourself.

name: 煮干中華そば 鈴蘭 (Niboshi Chuka Soba Suzuran)

ramen.chashu: Low-temperature cooked "rare" pork chashu, silky and tender enough to melt on the tongue; cut type/part not documented.

gf.note: Standard wheat noodles and a soy-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or broth option is documented.

noodles.notes: Default bowl comes with medium-thick curly (wavy) noodles; a low-hydration thin straight noodle option can also be chosen.

ramen.pantheon: A standout of Tokyo's modern niboshi (dried-sardine) ramen movement: a double soup layers a clean, bittersweet niboshi dashi over a long-simmered pork-and-chicken base, giving a bowl that hits hard with dried-fish aroma yet finishes with unusual clarity and refinement rather than the murky bitterness lesser niboshi bowls can have.
ramen.signature_bowl: 煮干中華そば (Niboshi Chuka Soba) — the shop's namesake bowl of double-soup dried-sardine ramen; a richer "濃厚" (extra-strong niboshi) version and a tsukemen are also signature orders.

vegan.note: Broth is built on niboshi (dried sardine) dashi combined with pork/chicken stock; no plant-based bowl is documented.

name: ラーメン二郎 新宿小滝橋通り店 (Ramen Jiro Shinjuku Kotakibashi-dori)

ramen.chashu: Signature Jiro "buta" — two thick, hand-portioned slabs of pork shoulder/belly, simmered until fork-tender; prized for sheer size and melt-apart texture rather than delicate slicing.

gf.note: Thick wheat noodles and a soy-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or broth option is offered.

noodles.notes: Extra-thick (gokubuto), flat-cut, dense and chewy Jiro-style noodles boiled firm by default; the shop is noted for moving to an even thicker cut around 2019. Unclear whether noodles are made in-house at this branch.

notable_toppings: yasai (mountain of boiled bean sprouts and cabbage)
abura (extra pork back fat, by request)
ramen.pantheon: This bowl belongs to Jiro-kei — the cult Tokyo lineage descended from the original Ramen Jiro in Mita, built around towering portions rather than refinement. Here the pork-bone broth is only lightly emulsified, giving a milder, back-fat-forward soup (rather than a heavy boiled-pork punch) carried by a soy tare, ladled over famously thick, dense, chewy noodles and slabs of fall-apart pork.
ramen.signature_bowl: Small Ramen (小ラーメン) — order yasai/niniku/abura to taste for the full Jiro experience, pork bone-shoyu broth piled with vegetables, garlic, and thick-cut chashu.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bones with pork back fat and pork chashu; no plant-based bowl is documented.

name: 上海飯店 (Shanghai Hanten)

noodles.notes: No documented detail on the noodles specifically; presumed standard machine-cut noodles typical of an old-school Tokyo neighborhood Chinese diner.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tanmen (¥650) — the shop's most-ordered noodle bowl, a salt-seasoned soup topped with stir-fried vegetables
notable_toppings: stir-fried mixed vegetables (cabbage, bean sprouts, carrot)

gf.note: Standard wheat noodles and a soy/salt-seasoned kitchen; no gluten-free options documented.

vegan.note: Broth and menu lean on pork and everyday Chinese-diner cooking; no plant-based broth option documented.

ramen.pantheon: A Showa-era neighborhood Chinese diner (machi-chuka) that has fed the Suidobashi crowds since the early 1960s, its tanmen is the humble, everyday face of Tokyo ramen — a cloudy, vegetable-laden salt broth rather than a chased regional style, built for comfort more than spectacle. It sits alongside the shop's well-loved gyoza and fried rice as an unpretentious downtown institution.

name: らぁ麺 鳳仙花 (Ra-men Hosenka)

ramen.chashu: Low-temperature (sous-vide style) cooked pork shoulder loin, thin-sliced and served rare-style — a delicate, tender cut rather than a fatty braised block.

gf.note: Standard wheat-and-kansui noodles with a soy-based salt tare; no gluten-free noodle or broth option is documented.

noodles.notes: Ramen bowl uses medium-thin noodles blended with whole-grain (zenryufun) flour; the tsukemen version uses a medium-thick flat noodle, also with whole-grain flour mixed in.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Tokyo original, not a traditional regional style: the broth is built entirely around kinmedai (golden eye snapper) bones, simmered into a golden, faintly sweet ara-dashi and seasoned with a pronounced shio tare and a snapper-infused aromatic oil. It is less about lineage than about pushing one fish as far as it will go — the shop even sends diners home with a snapper chazuke made from the last of the soup.
ramen.signature_bowl: 金目鯛らぁ麺 (Kinmedai Ra-men) — golden eye snapper ara-dashi ramen

vegan.note: Broth is a fish-bone (kinmedai) dashi and the signature toppings include pork chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 168点心飲茶＆薬膳鍋 渋谷ヒカリエ店 (168 Tenshin Yamucha & Yakuzen Nabe Shibuya H)

noodles.notes: No documented detail on the noodle itself beyond it being served in a sesame-flavored tantanmen; likely a standard Chinese-style wheat noodle but shape/thickness unconfirmed.

ramen.signature_bowl: 胡麻風味担々麺 (Sesame-Fragrance Tantanmen) — described by the shop as a bowl where a toasty, rich sesame aroma spreads throughout; also offered as part of a lunch set.

gf.note: No gluten-free or alternative-noodle option is documented; standard wheat noodles and soy-based seasoning are assumed for this Chinese-restaurant-style tantanmen.

vegan.note: No plant-based version of the tantanmen is documented; the restaurant's other signature dish (a mushroom-based medicinal hot pot) suggests some vegetable-forward cooking, but the noodle bowl itself has no confirmed vegan option.

ramen.pantheon: This isn't a gotōchi ramen-shop bowl but a Cantonese/Chinese dim-sum restaurant's take on tantanmen — a mild, toasty sesame-forward noodle soup rather than the fiery Sichuan original, served alongside handmade dumplings and a jujube-and-goji medicinal hot pot rather than in a dedicated ramen-ya setting.

name: 焼きあご塩らー麺 たかはし 新宿本店 (Yakiago Shio Ramen Takahashi, Shinjuku Main )

noodles.notes: Custom-milled straight thick wheat noodle (house blend nicknamed "Menu no Hinauta") chosen to let the wheat's own aroma and flavor come through against the delicate broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Yakiago Shio Ramen (焼きあご塩らー麺) — or the "Tokusei" special version loaded with extra toppings and a triple-chashu plate
ramen.chashu: Two cuts: thin-sliced pork shoulder loin, low-temperature cooked; and a diced, slow-simmered pork belly block — tender, delicate textures that don't fight the clear broth.

gf.note: Standard wheat ramen noodles and a soy-based shio tare; no gluten-free noodle or sauce alternative is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bone, chicken, and fish (grilled flying fish/niboshi) dashi, and the bowl is topped with pork chashu — no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: The house speciality is a broth built around yaki-ago — flying fish that's skewered, charcoal-grilled, then sun-dried — an ingredient with deep roots in Kyushu dashi cooking, layered here over a pork-and-chicken base and finished with a fish's own aromatic oil so each sip reads like biting straight into the grilled fish. It's a modern, clear-broth take on shio ramen built to showcase that single smoky, oceanic dashi rather than any one region's classic style.

name: うさぎ（神泉のらぁめん屋 うさぎ） (Usagi)

noodles.notes: Springy medium-thick noodles built to carry a thick sesame-based sauce for the tantanmen. The house's separate shoyu ramen instead uses fine, silky straight noodles milled from select domestic (Nagano-grown) wheat.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tantanmen, served wet or as shiru-nashi (dry/brothless) — the bowl this shop is known for. A concentrated, sesame-rich sauce built on minced pork is tossed with chewy noodles, with the numbing sansho/flower-pepper tingle (麻) and chili heat (辣) each dialed up separately from mild to fiery. Alongside it, the house also pours a quieter, clear amber shoyu ramen made from a whole-chicken-and-seafood dashi.
ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced pork shoulder (kata-rosu), lightly salted and cooked low-and-slow to a rosy, tender near-rare finish, then quickly seared (aburi-style) for a whisper of char aroma.

gf.note: Wheat noodles and a soy/sesame-based tare and sauce throughout the menu; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Broths and sauces are built on chicken, seafood dashi, and minced pork; no plant-based bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A tiny Shibuya back-alley spot that runs two kitchens in one bowl-case: a gentle, clear shoyu ramen simmered from whole chicken and seafood dashi in classic Tokyo style, and — the real draw — a fiery, sesame-creamy tantanmen you can order wet or completely dry (shiru-nashi), tuning your own numbing sansho buzz and chili heat to taste.

name: 舎鈴 新宿センタービル店 (Sharin — Shinjuku Center Building)

noodles.notes: In-house medium-thick straight noodles milled to show off raw wheat flavor, built to hold a heavy dipping sauce without going mushy; a rotating limited item uses an extra-thick noodle instead.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tsukemen (つけめん) — medium-thick wheat noodles served cold alongside a hot, seafood-forward pork-and-chicken dipping sauce; a limited "founding tsukemen" (創業つけめん) recreation of the parent brand's original 2005 recipe also shows up as a seasonal special.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu, sliced thin as a supporting topping rather than the bowl's centerpiece — the dipping sauce, not the pork, is the star. Exact cut/prep not confirmed in sources.

gf.note: Standard wheat tsukemen noodles and shoyu-based dipping sauce; no gluten-free noodle or tare option found.

vegan.note: Dipping sauce is built on seafood dashi plus pork and chicken stock with pork chashu; no plant-based broth option found.

ramen.pantheon: Sharin is the lighter little-sibling brand of Rokurinsha, the Tokyo Station shop credited with popularizing gyokai-tonkotsu (seafood-pork bone) dipping tsukemen in the mid-2000s — this bowl takes that dense double soup and dials it back into an everyday-eatable version, keeping the seafood punch up front while easing off the sheer heft of the original. You taste the lineage in the dipping sauce: fish-stock brightness riding on a pork-and-chicken base, built to be slurped daily rather than as an occasional gut-bomb.

name: 火鍋 小肥羊 新宿西口店 (Hinabe Xiaofeiyang Shinjuku Nishiguchi-ten)

gf.note: Standard wheat noodles; no gluten-free noodle or tare alternative is documented for this location.

noodles.notes: This shop is primarily a Chinese medicinal hot-pot specialist; noodles appear on the menu as a mala tantanmen dish and/or a noodle course finished in the hot-pot broth, but the specific noodle cut, thickness, and hydration are not documented in available sources.

ramen.pantheon: This is less a dedicated ramen counter than a Chinese medicinal hot-pot specialist that also plates a noodle bowl in its house broths — most notably a mala-spiced tantanmen-style noodle in the same chili-red, Sichuan-peppercorn-and-herb broth that anchors its hot pots. Its lineage runs to Inner Mongolian/Sichuan-style mala hot pot rather than any Japanese regional ramen tradition, so the numbing, beef-tallow richness is the point, not classic shoyu or tonkotsu depth.
ramen.signature_bowl: 薬膳麻辣担々麺 (medicinal mala tantanmen) — wheat noodles in the shop's herbal mala hot-pot broth; also offered as 小肥羊ラーメン, noodles finished directly in the diner's leftover hot-pot broth at the end of the meal.

vegan.note: Broth is built on beef tallow and an animal-based herbal stock, and the meal centers on meat/seafood hot-pot ingredients; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 台南担仔麺 新宿本店 (Tainan Tami Shinjuku Honten (Tainan Danzaime)

ramen.chashu: No sliced chashu — the bowl's heart is a Tainan-style minced pork topping (rousao), pork simmered down in soy sauce with garlic and shallot into a savory paste, ladled generously over the noodles in place of any chashu slice.

gf.note: Wheat noodles and a soy-based meat sauce; no gluten-free noodle or sauce option is documented.

noodles.notes: Thick wheat noodles are buried under a generous helping of Taiwanese-style minced pork topping and a Taiwan-style chili oil drizzle.

ramen.pantheon: This is Tainan danzai mian, not Japanese ramen — a light chicken-and-pork-bone broth built as a street-stall classic from Tainan, Taiwan, sweetened and deepened by a spoonful of soy-braised minced pork rather than sliced chashu. It's traditionally served as a small, palate-clearing bowl, and this Shinjuku shop leans into that light, savory character while adding its own kick of Taiwanese chili oil.
regional_style: Tainan danzai mian (Taiwanese, non-Japanese regional style)
ramen.signature_bowl: 担仔麺 (Tami-men / Tainan danzai noodles) — a light chicken-and-pork-bone broth bowl finished with the restaurant's original minced-pork sauce, traditionally sized as a small finishing dish.

vegan.note: Broth is chicken-and-pork-bone based and the signature topping is a pork mince sauce; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: つけ麺 五ノ神製作所 (Tsukemen Gonokami Seisakusho)

noodles.notes: In-house extra-thick straight noodles made with whole-wheat (zenryufun) flour, firmly water-rinsed for a chewy, resistant bite; pronounced wheat aroma, built to carry the dense dipping broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ebi Tsukemen (海老つけ麺) - extra-thick dipping noodles served with a bisque-like, ultra-rich shrimp dipping broth built on a pork-bone base
ramen.chashu: Sliced roasted pork loin fillet, lean and mild, serving as a supporting player rather than the star (a chicken-breast fillet alternative appears on some variant bowls)

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-based (whole-wheat blend) and the tare is shoyu (soy sauce); no gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: The signature broth is built on pork bone, chicken bone, and shrimp/seafood stock with a soy-based tare and meat chashu; no plant-based bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Tokyo tsukemen built around a shellfish-forward soul: a shrimp-and-seafood stock is folded into a pork-bone base until it turns bisque-thick and orange, so every dip tastes like concentrated ebi rather than plain tonkotsu. Extra-thick, whole-wheat noodles are built specifically to shoulder that dense, clinging dipping broth.
sources: https://ramen-daisuki-mormor987.com/2020/02/19/エビとトマトと小麦の絶妙なハーモニー：五ノ神製作所/

name: めんめん・かめぞう (Menmen Kamezou)

gf.note: Standard wheat ramen noodles and a wheat-based tare; no gluten-free alternative is documented.

noodles.notes: Very thin, straight, low-hydration noodles cooked firm (kata recommended); one free kaedama (noodle refill) comes with every tonkotsu bowl in classic Hakata fashion.

ramen.pantheon: A Hakata-lineage tonkotsu bowl reworked in a Japanese key: the pork-bone base is softened and rounded out with a seafood dashi, so the soup drinks less chalky-white and heavy than a classic Kyushu bowl while keeping the telltale free kaedama noodle refill. It's old-school Tokyo counter ramen — a quiet, unpretentious take on Hakata tonkotsu rather than a showy modern reinterpretation.
ramen.signature_bowl: Ajitama Hakata Tonkotsu Ramen (味玉博多とんこつ), with a free kaedama noodle refill

vegan.note: Broth is a pork-bone and seafood blend with pork chashu; no plant-based bowl is documented.

name: 油そば 春日亭 渋谷店 (Abura-soba Kasugatei Shibuya Ten)

ramen.chashu: Plain rectangular-cut pork chashu slices, simply prepared rather than char-grilled or fatty belly — a supporting topping rather than the bowl's centerpiece; the richness here comes mainly from the tare and rendered pork back fat rather than the meat.

gf.note: Standard wheat noodles with kansui and a wheat-based shoyu tare; no gluten-free noodle or sauce option is documented.

noodles.notes: Custom-milled 100% Hokkaido-wheat noodles made for the chain (not hand-pulled in-shop), thick and slightly curly with a springy, chewy bite; the surface is smooth enough to cling tightly to the tare when mixed.

notable_toppings: optional soft-boiled egg on select variants
ramen.pantheon: This bowl sits in Tokyo's abura-soba lineage — the soupless, dry-mixed noodle style born in the western Musashino suburbs — built on a two-day chicken-and-pork bone simmer reduced into a punchy kaeshi-style shoyu tare blended from Kagoshima and Kagawa soy sauces with niboshi, kombu, and shiitake. Rendered pork back fat scattered over thick, springy Hokkaido-wheat noodles gives every bite its glossy coating, meant to be sharpened at the table with vinegar and chili oil in classic abura-soba fashion.
ramen.signature_bowl: 鳥豚油そば (Tori-ton Abura-soba) — the shop's signature chicken-and-pork oil soba, its best-selling bowl

vegan.note: Broth is a chicken-and-pork bone simmer and the tare/chashu are animal-based; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 麺屋 我ガ 渋谷二丁目中央店 (Menya Gaga Shibuya Nichome Chuo)

ramen.chashu: Two thin-sliced pieces of lean pork loin chashu, cut in the classic thin Hakata style rather than thick braised chunks.

gf.note: Straight wheat noodles with kansui and a wheat-based tare system; no rice or alternative noodle option is documented, so celiac diners should assume it is not safe.

noodles.notes: Ultra-thin (goku-hosomen), low-hydration straight noodles in the Hakata mold, made from selected flour and rested/aged roughly a day before service; orderable at different firmness levels, turning crisp and springy when ordered firm.

notable_toppings: Housemade aged red spicy sauce served to stir in and change the broth's flavor partway through
Takana (pickled mustard greens) onigiri as a side
ramen.pantheon: A straight-up Hakata-lineage tonkotsu bowl, brewed from select pork bones for upward of 21 hours into a creamy, milky-white broth built to be clean and unfunky rather than gamey. The kitchen's roots trace back to the founding family of Japan's most famous tonkotsu chain, and it shows in the ultra-thin, low-hydration noodles and the aged red chili sauce diners stir in partway through the bowl to shift the broth from mellow pork-bone sweetness into a spicier register.
ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (the base tonkotsu bowl, or with ajitama/marinated egg added)

vegan.note: No plant-based broth or tare is documented; the bowl is built on a long-simmered pork bone broth, making it unsuitable for vegan diners.

name: 九月堂 (Kugatsudo)

ramen.chashu: House-made chashu (one slice in the standard bowl, two in the tsukemen); also served torched (aburi) and glazed with tare and sesame as a rice topping. Cut/cook style (loin vs. belly) not documented.

gf.note: Standard wheat noodles (sourced from an outside noodle maker) with a shoyu-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick noodles supplied by an outside noodle manufacturer (Mikawaya Seimen), not house-made; shape and hydration not documented.

ramen.pantheon: A neighborhood spin on Tokyo shoyu ramen built as a W-soup: pork bone, chicken, vegetable and black bean stock layered with a katsuobushi-and-niboshi Japanese dashi, seasoned with no chemical additives so the double broth carries its own natural depth. The kitchen pours it two ways from the same base — a lighter, seafood-forward assari and a thicker, chicken-and-vegetable-driven kotteri — letting the diner pick how far into the broth they want to go.
ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (choice of Assari — light, seafood/bonito-forward shoyu — or Kotteri — rich, chicken-and-vegetable-driven); the Special Tsukemen with double chashu and ajitama is also a house favorite.

vegan.note: Broth is a blend of pork bone, chicken and bonito/niboshi (fish) dashi; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 光醤 (Hikari Hishio)

noodles.notes: Thin, low-hydration Hakata-style noodles; firmness is customizable across roughly six levels (from soft to bari-kata/hard-boiled), paired with unlimited free kaedama refills.

ramen.signature_bowl: 光らーめん (Hikari Ramen) — the house tonkotsu shoyu bowl, topped with ajitama, chashu, negi, nori, moyashi and kikurage, eaten with unlimited free kaedama noodle refills until the pot runs dry
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu cut with a hearty balance of lean meat and fat, in the everyday Hakata-shop style rather than a char-grilled aburi cut

gf.note: Standard wheat kansui noodles and a shoyu-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare alternative found.

vegan.note: Broth is built on simmered pork bone with rendered pork back-fat; no plant-based broth option documented.

ramen.pantheon: A late-night slice of Hakata transplanted onto Dogenzaka: a lightly emulsified pork-bone broth darkened by a confident shoyu tare and finished with melted pork back-fat, paired with thin, low-hydration noodles you can dial from soft to bari-kata. It leans on the classic Hakata rhythm of small bowls and free kaedama refills rather than a single showpiece topping.

name: ラーメン二郎 新宿歌舞伎町店 (Ramen Jiro Shinjuku Kabukicho)

ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised pork slices (regular order comes with 2, "chashu" option piles on 4, doubled to 8) soaked and simmered in the shop's own pork broth; the Kabukicho branch's pork is often singled out as leaner and less fatty than at other Jiro locations, making it a gentler entry point to the style while still carrying a big, meaty bite.

gf.note: Standard wheat-flour noodles and a soy-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or sauce option is documented.

noodles.notes: In-house, ultra-thick, flat-cut wavy noodles with a firm, low-hydration chew and a strong wheat-flour aroma; they're made to be eaten fast before they soften in the broth.

notable_toppings: yasai (a mountain of boiled cabbage and bean sprouts)
karame (extra tare for a saltier bowl, by request)
ramen.pantheon: This bowl is a direct branch of the original Ramen Jiro lineage that essentially invented Tokyo's Jiro-kei cult: a thick, white, emulsified pork-bone broth cut with a punchy shoyu tare and buried under raw garlic, pork back-fat, and a mountain of cabbage and bean sprouts. The Kabukicho shop is known among Jiro regulars as one of the more approachable branches, with broth and chashu that run a shade less fatty than the intensely heavy originals, though it's still built as a late-night, high-calorie fuel stop rather than a delicate soup.
ramen.signature_bowl: Small (regular) Ramen, yasai mashi (extra vegetables) with ninniku (garlic) and abura (pork fat) — the classic Jiro call that shows off the full stack of cabbage, garlic, and fat over the thick noodles and broth.

vegan.note: Broth is pork-bone based and every bowl carries pork chashu and pork back-fat; no plant-based broth or protein option is documented.

name: すごい煮干ラーメン凪 新宿ゴールデン街店本館 (Sugoi Niboshi Ramen Nagi — Shinjuku Golden G)

ramen.chashu: Braised pork thigh (momo) chashu — lean, tender slices rather than fatty belly; the special bowl piles on four thick cuts.

gf.note: Standard wheat ramen noodles and a shoyu-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

noodles.notes: Nagi's proprietary medium-thick wavy noodle, described as hand-kneaded (temomi) with a chewy, mochi-like bite that grips the thick niboshi broth.

ramen.pantheon: This is the shop that ignited Tokyo's modern niboshi-ramen boom: a thunderously fishy bowl built by simmering 20-plus kinds of dried sardines into a cloudy, fat-slicked pork-and-chicken paitan until the broth tastes like you're chewing whole niboshi. The spent sardines are ground with lard into a house niboshi oil that gets ladled back on top, doubling down on the fish-bomb intensity that made this tiny Golden Gai stall a pilgrimage site.
ramen.signature_bowl: 特製すごい煮干ラーメン (Tokusei Sugoi Niboshi Ramen) — the special version of the shop's namesake ultra-concentrated niboshi bowl, loaded with extra chashu.

vegan.note: Broth is built on niboshi plus pork, chicken, and beef bones; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 国際秘羊館 (Kokusai Hiyoukan)

ramen.chashu: Shredded, slow-braised pork (belly-style) rather than sliced roast — soft and pull-apart, piled loose over the noodles instead of laid in rounds.

gf.note: Standard wheat-flour kansui noodles with a soy-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or broth option is documented.

noodles.notes: Thin, straight low-hydration noodles shipped in from a Fukuoka noodle maker in the classic Hakata style — quick-boiled for a firm, springy bite, with a free kaedama (noodle refill) offered.

notable_toppings: ajitama soft-boiled egg (on the tsukimi version)
ramen.pantheon: Officially a Genghis Khan (grilled lamb) specialist, this shop moonlights at weekday lunch as a serious Hakata-style tonkotsu counter — the broth is boiled from pork head bones alone and topped off day after day rather than started from scratch, chasing the funky, rich white soup the owner grew up eating at a food stall back home in Sasebo. Thin, straight Fukuoka-made noodles built for a fast, springy bite round out the classic Hakata silhouette.
ramen.signature_bowl: 豚骨ラーメン (Tonkotsu Ramen) — the shop's old-school Hakata-style pork bone ramen, the bowl its lunch reputation is built on.

vegan.note: Broth is boiled from pork head bones with pork fat rendered into the soup; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: つじ田 水道橋店 (Tsujita Suidobashi)

ramen.chashu: Housemade braised pork chashu, thick-cut; the special (tokusei) bowl doubles the portion. A hearty, meaty counterpoint rather than the headline act — the broth is the star here.

gf.note: Wheat-based custom noodles and a shoyu-leaning animal tare; no gluten-free noodle or broth option is documented.

noodles.notes: Custom-order noodles co-developed with Mikawaya Seimen: high-hydration, square-cut thick straight noodles with strong body and a mochi-mochi chew — closer in width to Higashi-Ikebukuro Taishoken than to ultra-thick jiro-style noodles.

ramen.pantheon: Tsujita is widely credited as the pioneer of Tokyo's noukou tonkotsu-gyokai (rich pork-bone-and-seafood) tsukemen — a thick, double-soup dip built from pork bone, chicken bone, dried seafood, kombu, and vegetables into something close to liquid umami concentrate, ladled over springy high-hydration noodles. The house ritual — eat it plain first, then bright with a squeeze of sudachi citrus, then dusted with centuries-old black shichimi pepper — turns one bowl into three distinct tastes before the dip is finished.
ramen.signature_bowl: 濃厚特製つけ麺 (Noukou Tokusei Tsukemen) — the deluxe version of the shop's pioneering rich tonkotsu-gyokai dipping noodles, loaded with extra housemade chashu and a soft ajitama egg.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bone, chicken bone, and dried-fish dashi throughout the chain's menu; no plant-based bowl is documented.

name: シンちゃんラーメン (Shin-chan Ramen)

ramen.chashu: Freshly hand-sliced pork chashu (切り立てチャーシュー) cut from multiple parts of the pig in the same bowl, so pieces range from fatty and sweet to leaner and clean-tasting; served in a generous, overflowing pile rather than a single uniform cut. A spicy chashu (辛味チャーシュー) version is also offered.

gf.note: Straight wheat noodles and a shoyu-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or broth option is documented.

noodles.notes: Flat, straight, high-hydration (多加水) noodles supplied by a Shinjuku noodle maker associated with the chan-kei genre; springy and slick with a broad, flat surface that carries extra broth to the mouth, boiled on the softer side.

ramen.pantheon: A standard-bearer of Tokyo's newer "chan-kei" ramen wave: a bowl brimming to the rim with clear pork chintan broth, its soul a deeply reduced but clean pork essence carried by a bold, dark shoyu tare rather than any heavy fattiness. Flat, slick high-hydration noodles and a mountain of fresh-sliced chashu turn what reads as a simple Tokyo shoyu bowl into an indulgent, chashu-forward feast.
ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (Chuka Soba) — the house classic, a shoyu pork-chintan bowl piled with abundant fresh-sliced chashu; the chashu-loaded もりチャーシュー (dip-style Mori Chashu) is also a popular order.

vegan.note: Broth is built on simmered pork bones/pork chashu and every bowl comes topped with pork; no plant-based broth is documented.

name: まぜそば 渋谷 チョップス (Mazesoba Shibuya Chops)

ramen.chashu: Braised pork arm/shoulder (ude) chashu — thick-cut, fatty and well-marbled, in the hearty Jiro-inspired style rather than a delicate aburi slice.

gf.note: Standard wheat noodles with kansui and a shoyu-based sauce; no gluten-free noodle or tare alternative is documented.

noodles.notes: Thick, chewy wavy noodles (some diners describe them as flat-cut) sized to order from 100g up to a 400g maximum; exact hydration level not documented.

notable_toppings: "Chops Bomber" seasoned crispy fried topping for texture
ramen.pantheon: A Shibuya mazesoba built on the DNA of Tokyo's legendary Jiro lineage: a hearty mound of thick, chewy noodles tossed in a pork-and-shoyu sauce with a garlicky, vegetable-piled Jiro-style presentation, but dialed back from a true Jiro bowl's oil load so the sweetness of the chicken fat comes through instead of getting buried.
ramen.signature_bowl: Shoyu Mazesoba — a Jiro-inspired mixed noodle bowl of thick wavy noodles tossed tableside in a pork-shoyu sauce finished with chicken fat, topped with a vegetable mound, chashu and menma, customizable for garlic, oil, and vegetable amounts.

vegan.note: Pork-based broth/sauce and animal chashu are core to every listed bowl; no plant-based or vegetable-broth option is documented.

name: 大勝軒まるいち 渋谷店 (Taishoken Maruichi Shibuya)

noodles.notes: House-made, egg-rich multi-water straight noodles served at 300g (pre-boil) for the tsukemen; pressed slowly for a dense, chewy, resilient bite typical of Taishoken-lineage tsukemen noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: 特製まるいちつけ麺 (Tokusei Maruichi Tsukemen) — the standard rich pork/chicken/seafood dipping broth upgraded with nori, a soft-simmered ajitama egg, and three thick-cut chashu slices.
ramen.chashu: Thick-sliced braised pork chashu is the standard; a seared "toro chashu" (fattier cut) is also offered as an add-on topping, and the everyday tsukemen comes with minced chashu bits mixed into the noodles.
notable_toppings: optional free yuzu zest and fish powder (gyokai-fun)

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-based with kansui and the tare is shoyu (soy sauce); no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The broth is built on pork bones, chicken frames, dried sardines, and mackerel flakes, and every bowl is topped with meat-based chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This bowl carries the bloodline of the legendary Higashi-Ikebukuro Taishoken — the shop that is credited with popularizing tsukemen across Japan — as its owner trained as the original master's last disciple. The dipping broth is built by double-simmering pork bones and chicken frames far longer than usual, then loading it with dried sardines and mackerel flakes for a thick, punchy, fish-deepened pork-and-chicken soul you dunk thick, chewy handmade noodles into.

name: 渋谷横丁 北海道食市 (Shibuya Yokocho Hokkaido Shokuichi)

gf.note: Standard wheat noodles and miso tare are used; no gluten-free or alternative-noodle option is documented for this shop.

noodles.notes: Menu names the dish "Sapporo miso ramen," a style traditionally built on thick, curly noodles to carry a rich miso broth, but this shop's specific noodle supplier/spec is not documented.

ramen.pantheon: Served as the shime (finishing) bowl inside a sprawling Hokkaido food-hall stall in Shibuya Yokocho, this is a Sapporo-style miso ramen — the archetype that put miso ramen on Japan's map, built around a hearty pork-rich broth and a bold miso tare, with a "rich miso curry ramen" variant on the menu for those who want it turned up further. It reads less as a ramen specialist's bowl and more as the noodle course at the end of a crab, uni, and jingisukan feast.
ramen.signature_bowl: Sapporo Miso Ramen (札幌味噌ラーメン); a "rich miso curry ramen" (濃厚味噌カレーラーメン) is also offered

vegan.note: Menu centers on pork/seafood broths and meat-heavy Hokkaido izakaya dishes (jingisukan, seafood bowls); no plant-based ramen option is documented.

name: ラーメン龍の家 新宿小滝橋通り店 (Ramen Tatsunoya Shinjuku Otakibashi-dori)

ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced pork loin chashu appears in the standard ramen bowls, but it plays second fiddle here — the shop's real signature protein is aburi (seared/grilled) pork motsu (offal), piled generously into the Shinjuku-exclusive tsukemen, giving crackly, fat-sweet bites you won't find in the regular ramen.

gf.note: No gluten-free or alternative-flour noodle option found; wheat noodles and a soy-based tare are standard across the menu.

noodles.notes: Custom medium-thick straight noodles made at the brand's own noodle factory (rooted in its Kurume home base); wheat-forward and firm enough to carry both the tonkotsu ramen and the thicker tsukemen dip.

notable_toppings: koge-negi/garlic aroma oil and back fat on the koku-mi bowl
ramen.pantheon: This bowl traces straight back to Kurume, the town widely credited as tonkotsu ramen's birthplace, and it plays that lineage two ways: a clean, "jun-mi" pork-bone soup that lets the boiled-head-bone broth speak for itself, and a heavier "koku-mi" version loaded with back fat, house miso, and burnt garlic-onion oil for a deeper, muskier hit. The soul of the Shinjuku shop, though, is its Tokyo-only tsukemen — a concentrated tonkotsu-shoyu dip stacked with seared pork offal and finished, izakaya-style, with a rice-porridge chaser instead of the usual soup dilution.
ramen.signature_bowl: Tsukemen Motsu — the Shinjuku-exclusive dipping noodles with seared pork offal (also orderable as "koku-mi tamago," the rich tonkotsu ramen with seasoned egg, if staying with soup-style ramen)

vegan.note: Pork-bone broth and pork offal are the shop's defining ingredients across the menu; no plant-based broth or bowl was found.

name: 純天然だしラーメン アートマサシヤ (Art Masashiya (Junten-nen Dashi Ramen))

ramen.chashu: Sliced pork chashu (cut and cooking method not documented) rather than the bowl's centerpiece — the broth is the star here.

gf.note: Standard wheat kansui noodles and a wheat-based soy-sauce tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

noodles.notes: Thin straight noodles made with an alum-free kansui, prized for a smooth, springy bite; whether they are made in-house is not documented.

ramen.pantheon: A quietly obsessive, additive-free dashi ramen: dried sardines, bonito flakes and kombu are simmered slow into a clear, faintly sour-umami broth, then rounded out with naturally brewed Shodoshima soy sauce and a lard-and-charred-scallion oil. It reads as an artisanal, health-minded take on old-school Tokyo shoyu ramen — dashi-forward and clean rather than fatty or bone-heavy.
ramen.signature_bowl: Umami-guchi (旨口) Shoyu Ramen — the shop's standard bowl, offered in three graduated intensities (umami-guchi, umami-guchi α, and the richer koi-guchi) so diners can dial the tare and aroma oil to taste.

vegan.note: Broth relies on fish dashi (niboshi, katsuobushi) and the aroma oil is pork lard-based; no plant-based bowl is documented.

name: スタミナラーメン鬼山 渋谷店 (Stamina Ramen Oniyama (Shibuya))

ramen.chashu: Not a classic braised block — thin-sliced pork (loin/belly) is stir-fried with garlic and onion and piled on top, in the "suta-man" stamina-ramen style rather than traditional chashu.

gf.note: Wheat-based thick noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

noodles.notes: Extra-thick, low-hydration noodles with a firm, chewy bite built to hold the heavy, sweet-soy pork broth and back fat — in the mold of the Mitaka "suta-man" stamina-ramen style.

ramen.pantheon: This bowl channels Tokyo's modern "suta-man" (stamina-full) boom sparked by Mitaka's cult favorite Suzuoni: a jet-black, sweet-soy pork broth loaded with melting back fat, raw garlic, and ginger, then buried under stir-fried pork, onion, and chives. It is less about broth nuance than about garlic-forward, fatty, stamina-building punch in every bite.
ramen.signature_bowl: スタミナラーメン (Stamina Ramen) — pork broth in a jet-black sweet soy tare, extra-thick chewy noodles, and a generous heap of back fat, garlic, ginger, onion, and chives.

vegan.note: Pork broth and pork toppings throughout; no plant-based broth or protein option is documented.

name: 黄金の塩らぁ麺 ドゥエ イタリアン 東急プラザ渋谷店 (Due Italian – Tokyu Plaza Shibuya (Ougon no )

ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu, generously portioned in the fully-loaded ("zenbu-nose") version of the golden shio ramen; specific cut and cooking method not documented. Note: on this shop's cheese-forward "ramen fromage" dish, chashu is swapped out entirely for thin-sliced prosciutto (generi ham) since the chef considers chashu a poor match for the melted cheese.

gf.note: A konnyaku (konjac) noodle swap is offered as a chef's low-carb/healthy alternative to the standard wheat noodle, which would be wheat-free — but the shio tare and any seasonings are not confirmed gluten-free, so celiac diners should ask staff directly rather than assume safety.

noodles.notes: Thin, taut straight noodles with a notably firm, springy bite (強めのコシ); a konjac-noodle option also exists as a low-carb swap. Hydration level and whether noodles are made in-house are not documented.

notable_toppings: Generous pork chashu (in the fully-loaded topping tier)
Ajitama (soft-seasoned egg, available as an add-on)
Prosciutto (in lieu of chashu, on the cheese-topped "fromage" ramen)
ramen.pantheon: This is modern, chef-driven "one bowl, full course" ramen rather than a heritage regional style: a golden, clean chintan built from chicken bone simmered with kombu and scallop for a rounded, savory-sweet depth, finished shio-style so the broth's own clarity and umami carry the bowl. The same kitchen is known citywide for pushing that base further into Italian territory — melting Gorgonzola-laced cream cheese and prosciutto into the noodles — but the Shibuya location's headline order is the straight golden shio version.
ramen.signature_bowl: Golden Shio Ramen (黄金の塩らぁ麦), especially the fully-loaded "zenbu-nose" topping version — a clear chicken-kombu-scallop broth seasoned with salt tare; the shop is also well known chain-wide for its cheese-and-prosciutto "ramen fromage."

vegan.note: No plant-based broth or bowl is documented; the signature broths are chicken/seafood-based and chashu or prosciutto are core toppings.

name: ラーメンまこと屋 渋谷センター街本店 (Ramen Makotoya Shibuya Center-gai Honten)

ramen.chashu: Pork chashu long-simmered (not seared) in the shop's own sauce until fork-tender, piled generously on the flagship bowl.

gf.note: Standard wheat ramen noodles and wheat-based tare (shoyu/miso); no gluten-free noodle or sauce option is documented.

noodles.notes: In-house low-hydration thin straight wheat noodles, aged roughly 48 hours, cut deliberately firm so they hold up against the thick beef-bone broth instead of going soft.

ramen.pantheon: This bowl belongs to a rare gyūkotsu (beef-bone) paitan lineage pioneered by this Osaka-born chain: whole beef bones are pressure-cooked for hours into a milky white broth that trades tonkotsu's funk for a cleaner, almost sweet depth. It's finished with a three-stage fried-garlic oil and, on request, a housemade spicy miso, with sibling bowls layering in seafood dashi or an aged soy-sauce-and-backfat riff for those who want more punch.
ramen.signature_bowl: Special Gyūjan Ramen (スペシャル牛じゃんラーメン) — a Shibuya-location-only bowl of the beef-bone paitan broth topped with a half-marinated ajitama and slow-braised chashu.

vegan.note: Broth is built from beef bones, with seafood, pork, or chicken elements layered into several menu variants, and the tares are animal-derived; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 三田製麺所 水道橋東京ドーム前店 (Mita Seimenjo Suidobashi Tokyo Dome-mae)

noodles.notes: In-house squared extra-thick straight noodles ("kaku-straight #12") made from a stone-milled wheat blend, springy and chewy with a somewhat high hydration that stands up to the concentrated dipping broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Nokou Tonkotsu Gyokai Tsukemen (rich pork-bone & seafood dipping noodles) — thick handmade noodles dunked in a long-simmered, heavily reduced pork-bone broth fortified with dried fish/sardine, topped with a shower of toasted fish powder; a "tokuno" (extra-concentrated) version and a garlic-and-back-fat "seiabura bancho" version are also offered, with free soup-dilution (sup-wari) service at the end.
ramen.chashu: Diced (kakugiri) braised pork chashu stirred into the dip, plus an optional single slice as part of the shop's topping set; exact cut not documented.

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-based and the tare is shoyu (wheat-containing); no gluten-free noodle or sauce option is documented.

vegan.note: The broth is built on long-simmered pork bone plus dried fish/sardine, with pork chashu and back-fat throughout the menu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This bowl sits in Tokyo's tsukemen lineage — thick, chewy noodles served apart from a scalding, drastically reduced dipping broth built by cooking pork bone and dried fish down to their limit. What you taste is concentration itself: a syrupy, umami-dense pork-and-seafood dip finished with a snowfall of toasted fish powder, built to cling to every strand of noodle.

name: Tokyo vege ramen veJin（ベジン） (Tokyo Vege Ramen veJin)

ramen.chashu: Soy-meat chashu ("Prime Soy Meat") — plant protein formed, marinated and seared to mimic braised pork belly chashu in look, flavor, and texture; no real meat used.

gf.note: Standard wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles are used; no rice-flour or gluten-free noodle option is documented for this shop.

noodles.notes: Diner accounts describe medium noodles with a pleasant chew and bouncy texture; exact shape/hydration not documented.

ramen.pantheon: This bowl borrows the silhouette of Hakata-style tonkotsu paitan without any bone in the pot: a shiitake, napa cabbage, kelp and scallion dashi is slow-simmered and reduced into a cream-thick, opaque white soup that mimics pork-bone richness, then split into shoyu, shio and miso finishes with soy-meat "chashu" standing in for pork belly.
ramen.signature_bowl: Vege Paitan Shoyu Special (Vege白湯醤油スペシャル) — the shop's flagship soy-meat-chashu bowl in the cream-thick plant-based paitan broth.
