vegan.note: The entire menu is 100% plant-based (no meat, fish, egg, or dairy) — the whole soup line and soy-meat chashu are vegan by design, not a special-order option.

name: らーめん はやし (Hayashi)

ramen.chashu: Sauce-braised pork loin, cut thick — firm-textured, not fatty aburi-style, meant to hold its own against the broth rather than melt into it.

gf.note: Wheat noodles from an outside noodle maker (Mikawaya Seimen) and a shoyu-based tare; no gluten-free or alternative-noodle option is documented.

noodles.notes: Custom low-hydration medium straight noodles supplied by Mikawaya Seimen — firm, chewy bite designed to stand up to the concentrated broth rather than a soft, high-hydration strand.

ramen.pantheon: A Shibuya benchmark of the no-added-seasoning (mukachō) school of modern Tokyo ramen: pork bone and chicken are simmered alongside kombu, niboshi, dried shrimp and bonito into a double broth that looks like it should be heavy but drinks clean and light, its umami concentrated rather than oily. A whisper of fresh yuzu zest blooms on the palate as the bowl cools, giving the shoyu broth a citrus lift you rarely find on a pork-based soup.
ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (shoyu) — the shop's single namesake bowl, taken plain or dressed up with chashu and ajitama; a secret shio (salt) version can be ordered by request.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bone, chicken and dried-fish/bonito dashi with a meat-based chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 鶏そば・ラーメン Tonari (Torisoba Ramen Tonari)

ramen.chashu: Moist, gently-cooked chicken chashu (not pork) — sliced chicken breast/thigh is the shop's signature protein, playing off its chicken-bone broth.

gf.note: Standard wheat ramen noodles and shoyu/shio tare; no rice or konjac noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented.

noodles.notes: Uses noodles from an outside noodle specialist (Mikawaya), thin straight style paired with the clear chicken broth; some paitan and tsukemen bowls may use a different cut.

notable_toppings: seasonal oyster or seafood toppings on limited-run bowls
ramen.pantheon: A modern Tokyo tori-soba built on chicken bones simmered gently with kombu and a whisper of bonito instead of pork — clean, additive-free broth where you taste chicken and dashi rather than fat. Its real signature move is borrowing from the oyster bar next door, folding fresh oysters into wontons, shio soba, and tsukemen for a Shibuya-only twist on the classic clear chicken bowl.
ramen.signature_bowl: 白醤油鶏そば (Shiro-shoyu Torisoba) — clear chicken broth built from kombu dashi and chicken bones simmered with aromatics for about two hours, finished with white soy sauce and a light touch of bonito.

vegan.note: Broth is chicken-and-dashi based across the menu; no plant-based bowl is documented.

name: 北海道名物らー麺 えびそば 一幻 新宿店 (Hokkaido Meibutsu Ramen Ebisoba Ichigen Shin)

ramen.chashu: Slow-braised pork belly chashu, marinated in a house shoyu tare — a soft, savory foil rather than the headline; the shrimp broth is the star.

gf.note: Standard wheat ramen noodles and wheat-based tare across all three flavors (shio/miso/shoyu); no rice or konjac noodle substitute is documented.

noodles.notes: Custom-made noodles offered in two cuts: a thick noodle designed to cling to and soak up the shrimp broth (the house recommendation), or a thinner noodle for a cleaner, lighter read on the shrimp stock.

ramen.pantheon: Ichigen is less a regional lineage than a genre of its own: born in Sapporo's Susukino nightlife strip, it built its name on daily-simmered sweet-shrimp heads finished with a wok-fried shrimp oil and pork back-fat, then let diners dial the funk by blending that shrimp stock with a pork-bone base. The result is a lush, orange-tinted shrimp broth — a distinctly Hokkaido specialty rarely found built this way anywhere else.
ramen.signature_bowl: Ebi-miso "Ajiwai" (shrimp miso, deepened with pork-bone broth) on thick noodles — the shop's richest, most emblematic bowl, where the sweet-shrimp broth is thickened further with a tonkotsu blend.

vegan.note: Broth is built from simmered shrimp heads and (for the ほどほど/あじわい strengths) pork bone and pork back-fat; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 北ノ醤油チーホー 水道橋店 (Kita no Shoyu Chi-Ho (Suidobashi))

ramen.chashu: Simple, home-style sliced pork loin chashu (a plain, "ramen-shop diner" style rather than a fancy aburi or slow-braised showpiece) — a full chashu-topped version is offered as a step-up bowl.

gf.note: Standard wheat wavy noodles and a soy-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

noodles.notes: Pale-yellow, high-hydration medium-thick curly noodles from an outside noodle maker (Kanno Seimen), with a kansui-forward aroma and chewy bite reminiscent of Sapporo-style noodles.

notable_toppings: stir-fried ground pork and bean sprouts (moyashi itame) wok-tossed with garlic and ginger
ramen.pantheon: A modern Tokyo shoyu bowl built on a 100 percent pork-bone stock (no seafood dashi), finished tableside with a wok of garlic, ginger, minced pork, and bean sprouts stir-fried and folded straight into the broth for a roasted, almost sizzling aroma. The curly, medium-thick noodles nod to Sapporo ramen, so what lands in the bowl reads as Hokkaido's brawny, stir-fried richness poured over a sharp, old-school Tokyo shoyu backbone.
ramen.signature_bowl: 北ノ醤油ラーメン (Kita no Shoyu Ramen), 1,100 yen — the flagship 100%-pork-bone shoyu bowl with the wok-fried pork-and-bean-sprout topping

vegan.note: Broth is 100% pork-bone and topped with stir-fried ground pork; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 元祖油堂 宮益坂店 (Ganso Aburado Miyamasuzaka)

noodles.notes: A proprietary noodle blending ramen flour with pasta flour, reportedly refined over eight years; described by diners as thick and notably chewy/mochi-mochi with a glossy surface.

ramen.signature_bowl: Abura Soba (油そば) — the shop's core, soupless noodle bowl tossed tableside in a fragrant, slightly sweet shoyu-kaeshi sauce blended with seasoning oil; noodle portion can be upsized free from 150g up to a 300g extra-large. Combo versions add parmesan cheese and egg yolk (+A), or extra meat with fried onion and egg yolk (+B/+C).
ramen.chashu: Minced/crumbled pork chashu scattered through the noodles rather than sliced braised medallions — built as a textural topping alongside the other mix-ins rather than a centerpiece slab.
notable_toppings: extensive self-serve condiment bar: rayu chili oil, vinegar, extra kaeshi, fish powder, curry powder, aonori, black pepper, sesame, minced garlic, minced onion, red pickled ginger

gf.note: Standard wheat-flour noodles and a soy-based shoyu tare with no rice or konjac noodle alternative documented — not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Built around pork chashu and a blended tare/oil base; no plant-based broth or protein-free bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is Tokyo-style abura soba — a soupless bowl where noodles are tossed tableside in a sweet-savory shoyu kaeshi and seasoning oil instead of swimming in broth, so all the character lives in how that sauce coats each strand. The shop leans into the genre's playful side, handing diners a dozen-plus condiments and free noodle upsizing so the bowl gets tuned to taste with every bite.

name: まぜそば七 (Mazesoba Shichi)

noodles.notes: Described across sources as chewy, medium-thick noodles; four size options (120g/170g/250g/340g) all priced the same, so diners can scale portion freely.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shoyu mazesoba topped with charcoal-grilled chashu — mix thoroughly with the crispy fried onion, then customize table-side with vinegar, housemade chili oil, pepper, and minced garlic as you eat.
ramen.chashu: Pork chashu simmered low and slow until tender, then finished directly over charcoal for a smoky, charred exterior — the shop's signature "aburi" (charcoal-grilled) treatment rather than a plain braise.

gf.note: No gluten-free option found; noodles are standard wheat and the shoyu/shio sauces are wheat-based, as at essentially all mazesoba shops.

vegan.note: No plant-based option found; the signature bowl is built around pork chashu and an animal-based sauce, and no vegan alternative is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A Shibuya mazesoba specialist that skips the soup entirely, coating chewy noodles in a concentrated shoyu or clam-and-kelp shio sauce and a garlicky housemade aromatic oil, then piling on smoky charcoal-grilled chashu. It sits in the mix-it-yourself abura-soba/mazesoba lineage, with table-side vinegar, chili oil, and garlic meant to change the bowl's character bite by bite.

name: 鶏そば かぐら屋 (Torisoba Kaguraya (Suidōbashi Honten))

ramen.chashu: Chicken chashu served two ways — dark thigh meat and a low-temperature (sous-vide) cooked breast kept deliberately moist and tender, in place of the usual pork.

gf.note: Standard wheat ramen noodles and a wheat-based shoyu tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

noodles.notes: Medium-fine straight noodles with a light, springy bite designed to carry the thick chicken broth without going soft.

ramen.pantheon: This bowl rides Tokyo's tori-paitan wave: chicken bones and vegetables simmered for hours into a milky, collagen-rich broth that gives pork tonkotsu's silky heft without the pork, finished with a ten-ingredient salt tare built around Italian and Seto Inland sea salts, scallop, and kelp. A clearer, golden chintan chicken soup and a tomato-salt variant sit alongside it for drinkers who want something lighter.
ramen.signature_bowl: 濃熟鶏そば (Nōjuku Torisoba) — the shop's deeply matured, milky-white chicken paitan ramen

vegan.note: Broth is chicken-based and chashu is chicken; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 麺堂にしき 新宿歌舞伎町店 (Mendo Nishiki Shinjuku Kabukicho)

noodles.notes: Fine, straight, low-hydration noodles supplied by Mikawaya Seimen (a specialty noodle maker); one source describes them as house-made, so in-house vs. outsourced production is unconfirmed.

ramen.signature_bowl: Kamodashi Shoyu Ramen (鴨だし醤油ラーメン) — a clear duck-and-kelp broth deepened with kombu-mizu (kelp water) and a slightly sweet shoyu tare; also offered as tsukemen with a wasabi-and-citrus finish.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut duck (kamo) chashu, seared/roasted (aburi-style) rather than braised — the tsukemen version is noted for an especially high-quality cut.
notable_toppings: sansho (Japanese pepper) on the sansho ramen

gf.note: Standard wheat noodles and a wheat-derived shoyu tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Broths are built on duck and seafood/kombu dashi with duck chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern, craft-style shoyu ramen built on an uncommon base — duck (kamo) stock layered with kombu dashi in place of the usual pork or chicken — giving a clean, clear soup with a refined, savory-sweet depth rather than heavy fattiness. It leans contemporary Tokyo original rather than regional classic, with a sansho (Japanese pepper) version and a kombu-mizu duck tsukemen rounding out the line-up.

name: 十味や (Toomiya (Tomiya))

noodles.notes: Medium-thick, curly (chijire) wheat noodles typical of Sapporo-style miso ramen for the regular bowl; a thicker noodle is used for the tsukemen to stand up to the rich dipping sauce. Exact hydration/handmade status not documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: Kōkō-umi Awase Miso Ramen (濃厚合わせ味噌らーめん) — the shop's rich blended-miso bowl, combining red and white miso, and its best-selling item; red miso, white miso, and a spicy miso version are also offered.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly chashu, served meltingly tender ("とろ"/torotoro) rather than seared or lean loin-style.

gf.note: Wheat-based noodles with kansui and a soy/wheat-containing miso tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

vegan.note: No plant-based broth or vegan bowl is documented; pork belly chashu and miso tare (which typically incorporates animal-based stock/fat in this style) are standard.

ramen.pantheon: A Nishi-Shinjuku miso specialist tracing back to 1971, when it opened as one of Tokyo's early standard-bearers for Hokkaido-style (Sapporo) miso ramen; today it centers its bowls on an original "jūmi" (十味, "ten flavors") spice blend layered over red, white, and blended miso tare. The soup itself — its base broth composition — was not independently confirmed in available sources, but reviewers consistently describe the house blended-miso bowl as rich yet fully drinkable.

name: 中華麺店 喜楽 (Chuka Menten Kiraku)

noodles.notes: Thick noodles (some accounts describe them as flat-cut) that stand up to the fried-scallion shoyu broth; exact hydration and whether they're handmade in-house aren't documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu Wonton Noodles (チャーシューワンタン麺) — the shop's clear pork-and-chicken shoyu broth piled with fried scallions, plump generously filled wontons, and sliced chashu; regulars also order the wontons alone in bulk.
ramen.chashu: Simple sliced roast/braised pork chashu — a supporting player rather than the star; no specific cut or aburi-char technique documented.

gf.note: Standard wheat ramen noodles and a soy-based (wheat-containing) tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built from simmered pork and chicken bones with meat-based toppings (chashu, wontons); no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A Shibuya time capsule since 1952: a clear, gently pork-and-chicken shoyu broth given its whole personality by a torrent of fried scallions, the classic Tokyo old-school chuka-soba move that turns a simple soy soup smoky-sweet. The bowl to get is stacked with plump, generously filled wontons alongside the noodles.

name: 萬馬軒 新宿西口本店 (Manbaken Shinjuku Nishiguchi Honten)

noodles.notes: Custom-ordered medium-thick straight noodles with a chewy, springy bite designed to hold up against the thick miso soup; described as higher-hydration for a glossy, chewy texture.

ramen.signature_bowl: Negi Miso Chashu Men (ネギ味噌チャーシューメン) — a heaping mound of finely sliced scallions over the shop's concentrated miso ramen
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised pork (belly-style) slices, served substantial enough that they continue to soften and render in the hot broth as you eat.
notable_toppings: heaping pile of finely sliced negi (scallions)

gf.note: Standard wheat noodles and a soy-based miso tare; no gluten-free noodle or menu option found.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork and chicken bones with meat chashu as the signature topping; no plant-based broth option documented.

ramen.pantheon: Manbaken has staked its identity since 1988 on one thing: concentrated, punchy miso ramen, simmering pork and chicken bones down into a thick, clinging broth blended with a proprietary mix of misos. It's Tokyo kotteri-style miso pushed toward the extreme — a soup dense enough to coat the chewy noodles and its trademark mountain of scallions with real weight and heat.

name: つけそば 神田 勝本 (Tsukesoba Kanda Katsumoto)

noodles.notes: Served as a dual noodle plate from Asakusa Kaikaro: a supple, chewy medium-thick noodle alongside a firm, low-hydration thin noodle with a subtle crimp, letting one dip yield two different textures.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei (Special) Shoyu Tsukesoba — clear niboshi-shoyu dipping sauce with the shop's signature two-noodle plate, chashu, and seasoned egg.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly, cooked to a tender, almost melting texture while still holding a satisfying bite; the shop is also known for a separately-ordered torched (aburi) pork belly rice side.

gf.note: Wheat noodles and a shoyu-based tare; no gluten-free or alternative-noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on niboshi (dried sardine), chicken, and bonito-type dashi with pork chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A refined, almost consomme-clear niboshi-shoyu dipping sauce from a chef with decades of French kitchen training, built to punch above its delicate look. It breaks from the thick, pork-and-fish-heavy tsukemen mold by pairing that light, concentrated dip with two contrasting noodle textures in the same bowl.

name: つけ麺・らーめん 波海拉 (Namiura (Tsukemen & Ramen))

gf.note: Standard wheat noodles and soy-based tare with no gluten-free option documented.

noodles.notes: Whole-grain-flecked straight noodle with a firm, chewy bite and a faint toasted-bran flavor, built to stand up to the thick dipping broth.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Shinjuku dipping-noodle bowl that fuses the classic pork-bone-and-seafood tsukemen lineage popularized around the station with a spicier, Sichuan-pepper-tinged twist, alongside a separate niboshi-shoyu ramen for drinkers of clear, dried-sardine-forward broth. The tsukemen's thick, creamy dip and firm bran-flecked noodles sit squarely in Tokyo's contemporary gyokai-tonkotsu dipping-noodle tradition.
ramen.signature_bowl: つけ麺 粋 (Tsukemen Sui) — the house tsukemen: firm, thick, whole-grain-flecked noodles dunked in a middling-rich pork-bone-and-seafood dipping broth, with chashu, menma, and nori plated on the side so diners can add them to the dip at their own pace.

vegan.note: Broths are pork-bone/seafood or dried-sardine based with no plant-based bowl documented.

name: 炙り味噌らーめん 麺匠 真武咲弥 渋谷店 (Aburi Miso Ramen Mensho Shinbu Sakiya (Shibu)

noodles.notes: Aged (jukusei) medium-thick curly wheat noodles are standard; a gluten-free brown rice noodle can be swapped in on request.

ramen.signature_bowl: Aburi Miso Ramen — a secret tare blending Shinshu white miso and Hokkaido red (koitten) miso with over a dozen ingredients, scorched under an open flame for char and aroma, then stirred into a clear pork/chicken/fish broth.
ramen.chashu: Pork chashu served two ways in the same bowl — thin slices plus diced chunks — the blowtorch is applied to the miso tare itself rather than the meat.

gf.note: A gluten-free brown rice noodle swap is offered along with gluten-free fried chicken and dumplings, but ask staff to confirm — the default noodles and tare are wheat-based.

vegan.note: Dedicated vegan miso, vegan spicy miso, and vegan shoyu ramen bowls are on the menu with plant-based broth and toppings — flag it when ordering, since the standard bowls use a meat-based broth and pork chashu.

ramen.pantheon: This is Sapporo's blowtorched miso ramen transplanted to Shibuya's late-night strip: a secret tare blending Shinshu white miso and Hokkaido red miso is seared under an open flame before it hits the bowl, giving the soup a caramelized, smoky edge over a clear pork-chicken-fish broth.

name: 麺屋ぬかじ (Menya Nukaji)

noodles.notes: Made with Hokkaido wheat flour and Okinawan salt; described as not-too-thick and smooth-slurping, almost like thick spaghetti against the gravy-like dip.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei (Special) Tsukemen — the W-soup pork-bone and dried-fish dipping noodles loaded with every topping
ramen.chashu: Rolled pork belly, braised low and slow in a white-soy, mirin, sake, garlic, ginger and onion tare until tender but not falling apart — a homestyle braise rather than a char-grilled aburi piece.
notable_toppings: ajitama (seasoned soft-boiled egg, on the tokusei/ajitama versions)

gf.note: Standard wheat tsukemen noodles with a shoyu-based dip; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The signature dip is a pork-bone broth blended with bonito, mackerel and dried horse mackerel dashi, plus pork chashu — no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A Shibuya tsukemen built on a W-soup marriage of long-simmered pork bone broth and a smoky, multi-fish dashi (bonito, mackerel, dried horse mackerel), yielding a thick, gravy-like dip you scoop up with springy noodles rather than sip. It sits in Tokyo's modern tonkotsu-gyokai tsukemen wave — prized less for shock richness than for a deep, layered umami balance, capped off with a free-flowing egg-rice service to finish the leftover dip.

name: らーめん 鴨to葱 イイトルミネ新宿店 (Ramen Kamo to Negi - EATo Lumine Shinjuku)

noodles.notes: Flat, thin, straight noodles using a custom blend of domestic wheat plus whole-grain flour.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei Kamo Ramen (special duck ramen) — the clear duck shoyu broth topped with duck confit, wonton, seasoned egg, and bamboo shoots; the duck dipping noodles (kamo tsuke-soba) are the other house specialty.
ramen.chashu: Duck confit — the shop butchers whole domestic duck (sourced seasonally from producers around Japan) in house and slow-confits the meat, using the same bird for both the broth and the chashu; no pork is used.
notable_toppings: two selected varieties of seasonal Japanese scallion (chosen daily from three regional types)

gf.note: Standard wheat ramen noodles with a soy-sauce-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or sauce option is documented.

vegan.note: The broth is built entirely on simmered duck bones with duck fat rendered into the soup, and the signature topping is duck confit — no plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A one-ingredient-family purist's bowl: the broth is built from nothing but duck, allium (scallion and other alliums swapped seasonally by region), and water, simmered low for roughly two days into a limpid, soy-seasoned chintan. It reads delicate and clean on the first sip, then the duck's savory depth blooms in the aftertaste — a distinctly modern Tokyo take on shoyu ramen built around a single, deeply worked ingredient rather than a classic regional lineage.

name: 七転八起 (Shichiten Hakki)

ramen.chashu: No classic sliced chashu — the pork/meat topping here is served as chunky braised meat pieces ("gorogoro" pork chunks) alongside a separate thin-sliced marinated pork, rather than a traditional rolled loin or belly slice.

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-based and the triple stock is finished with a soy-based, miso-leaning tare; no gluten-free noodle or broth option is documented.

noodles.notes: Extra-thick, chewy curly noodles from a specialist noodle mill (Mikawaya Seimen), tinted yellow with gardenia (kuchinashi) and made to stand up to the bold, spicy broth.

ramen.pantheon: A modern, spice-forward original from the operators behind one of Tokyo's celebrated niboshi ramen shops: a chicken-pork-seafood triple stock kept light and clear, then charged with a customizable, numbing-spicy miso-leaning tare that climbs through fifteen heat levels. It's less about regional lineage and more about turning up the fire — thick, chewy noodles built to carry the heat and the meat.
ramen.signature_bowl: 七転八起麺 — the shop's namesake bowl: chicken, pork, and seafood triple soup with a customizable spicy tare (adjustable from mild up to fifteen heat levels), thick chewy noodles, chunky braised pork, and a seasoned soft egg. Also served tsukemen-style.

vegan.note: The signature triple soup is built on chicken, pork, and seafood stock and every bowl is loaded with meat toppings; no plant-based broth or bowl is documented.

name: 松太郎 新宿小滝橋通り店 (Matsutaro Shinjuku Kotakibashi-dori)

ramen.chashu: Sliced pork loin (rosu) chashu, braised and well-seasoned — reviewers note it's tender, juicy and the standout topping on an otherwise simple bowl.

gf.note: Standard wheat ramen noodles with kansui and a soy-based shoyu tare; no gluten-free noodle or sauce alternative is offered.

noodles.notes: Medium-thin, springy noodles made from Hokkaido wheat at the company's own noodle factory; praised for a firm, chewy (tsuru-shiko) bite that holds up in the light broth.

ramen.pantheon: A no-frills Tokyo-style shoyu/shio bowl built on a clear chicken-and-seafood double soup — niboshi and kombu dashi laid under a blend of four soy sauces gives it that old-fashioned, clean-finishing shoyu ramen character rather than the thick tonkotsu richness found at many rivals nearby. It's an everyday, budget-friendly bowl meant to be eaten often rather than a showpiece bowl.
ramen.signature_bowl: Shoyu Ramen (¥680) — the shop's flagship bowl, a four-soy-sauce blend over a chicken-and-seafood dashi with sliced chashu and menma; order it with the ajitama add-on for the fuller ¥780 version.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken/animal and niboshi (dried sardine) dashi with pork chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 七婆串串香 (Qipo Chuanchuanxiang)

gf.note: Standard wheat-based noodles in the weekday lunch tantanmen set; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

noodles.notes: No documented detail on the noodle itself. The tantanmen appears only as part of a weekday lunch (11:00-15:00) set paired with mapo rice, not as an a la carte ramen bowl.

ramen.pantheon: This is fundamentally a Chengdu-born chuan chuan xiang skewer hot pot house, not a ramen shop — its real soul is the dual broth diners dip skewers into: a mellow chicken-bone paitan (baitang) and a numbing Sichuan mala broth loaded with huajiao peppercorn and chili oil. The tantanmen surfaces only on the weekday lunch set alongside mapo rice, borrowing that same mala heat rather than standing as a dedicated ramen bowl.
ramen.signature_bowl: Weekday lunch-only tantanmen + mapo rice set — a mala-spiced noodle plate riding on the house's Sichuan broth; the skewer hot pot itself, not this bowl, is what the restaurant is actually built around.

vegan.note: Broths are chicken-bone (paitan) or meat-based mala stock, and the hot pot centers on meat/seafood skewers; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: 豚ノヴァ零-zero- 神田水道橋店 (Butanova Zero Kanda-Suidobashi)

noodles.notes: Jiro-inspired extra-thick noodles with a firm, low-broth-absorption 'patsu-patsu' snap; some accounts describe them as flat-cut, others as slightly wavy, so exact shape is not fully certain.

ramen.signature_bowl: 特製汁なし (Tokusei Shirunashi / Special Soupless), roughly 1,280 yen — the loaded flagship bowl piled with extra chashu and toppings
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut, chunky block pork chashu (not thin slices) with a seared/aburi finish for aromatic char, balancing firm meat against tender fat; extra pork ('豚増し') can be added.

gf.note: Standard wheat noodles with kansui and a soy-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or sauce option found.

vegan.note: Built entirely around pork-based tare, pork back fat, and pork chashu with no documented plant-based bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A Tokyo Jiro-kei bowl in its soupless (shirunashi/mazesoba) form, carrying the DNA of the cult favorite 豚星 via its owner's training lineage — extra-thick, snappy Yamamoto-seimen-style noodles get tossed in a soy-pork tare and hot pork back fat, then buried under chunky seared chashu for a junky, garlic-optional bowl built for volume eating.

name: 麺屋武蔵 武骨外伝 (Menya Musashi Bukotsu Gaiden)

ramen.chashu: Sliced roast pork chashu described by reviewers as tender and well-seasoned; exact cut/method (belly vs loin, aburi-seared vs braised) not documented for this branch.
notable_toppings: special thick shoyu or spicy miso "an" paste stirred into the dip

noodles.notes: Chewy, springy medium-thick straight noodles with a smooth, slippery throat-feel (nodogoshi), characteristic of the Menya Musashi group's proprietary wheat blend; noodle portions up to 1kg are offered at no extra charge.

ramen.signature_bowl: Nokou (Gaiden) Kuro Tsukemen (濃厚外伝黒つけ麺) — the rich black dipping noodles, finished with the shop's original thick soy-sauce-based "an" paste and a soft-boiled egg.

gf.note: Wheat noodles with kansui and a wheat-based shoyu/miso tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built from pork bone, chicken, crab, niboshi (dried sardine) and katsuobushi (bonito flakes), with meat chashu as standard; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A flagship offshoot of Menya Musashi, one of the houses that helped define Tokyo's modern tsukemen scene — the dipping broth fuses pork-and-chicken richness with crab and niboshi/katsuobushi seafood dashi into one thick, umami-dense soup. The signature bowl goes a step further, folding the noodles in a glossy, paste-like soy sauce "an" so the dish sits somewhere between classic tsukemen and a mazesoba-style toss.

name: らぁ麺 はやし田 新宿本店 (Raamen Hayashida Shinjuku Honten)

noodles.notes: Straight noodles milled from a custom blend of wheats (including whole-grain flour) for a clean, wheat-sweet bite and smooth swallow; exact hydration not documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei Shoyu Ra-men (特製醤油らぁ麺) — the shop's flagship bowl, dressed with double chashu and ajitama.
ramen.chashu: Two kinds served together: low-temperature-cooked pork shoulder loin and low-temperature-cooked chicken breast, both left moist and tender rather than seared or heavily fatty.

gf.note: Standard wheat-flour kansui noodles and a soy-based shoyu tare; no rice, konjac, or other GF noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The broth is built by simmering whole duck and Oyama-dori chicken, finished with chicken-and-duck fat, and the bowl carries pork/chicken chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Tokyo shoyu bowl built entirely from poultry: duck and premium Oyama-dori chicken are simmered into a clear, deep-amber chintan broth, its surface glossed with a generous layer of chicken-and-duck fat that carries the caramelized soy tare's sweetness through to the finish. It reads as a refined, all-poultry take on Tokyo-style shoyu ramen rather than a classic regional lineage bowl.

name: つけ麺 ズッパ 水道橋店 (Tsukemen Zuppa (Suidobashi))

ramen.chashu: No meat at all — chunks of seasoned soy meat (textured vegetable protein) simmered right into the dipping broth stand in for chashu, backed by fried lotus root and fried okra tempura piled on the noodles.

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-based (described as a whole-grain wheat, high-hydration thick noodle) with no rice or konjac alternative documented; no gluten-free accommodation found.

noodles.notes: Chewy, mochi-mochi high-hydration thick noodle with a slight curl (太やや縮れ麺), served in a standard 270g portion for the tsukemen; whole-grain wheat flour.

ramen.pantheon: A defiantly plant-only tsukemen: instead of pork tonkotsu, the dipping broth is a thick, paitan-style purée of simmered vegetables laced with tomato and miso-deep umami, studded with soy meat in place of chashu — proof that an entirely animal-free bowl can still hit heavy and indulgent.
ramen.signature_bowl: Tsukemen (つけ麺, ¥980) — thick vegetable dipping broth with tomato and miso-like depth, soy meat, and a vegan/vegetarian-adjustable topping spread.

vegan.note: Fully plant-based kitchen — no meat or fish used in any broth. The tsukemen and soy-meat ramen/tantanmen line are naturally vegan; regular noodles/toppings can include egg (e.g. œuf mayo), so ask for the vegan version to have it omitted.

name: らーめん 鴨to葱 渋谷宮益坂店 (Ramen Kamo to Negi Shibuya Miyamasuzaka)

noodles.notes: Custom-order noodles from Mikawaya Seimen made with whole-wheat flour, giving visible bran flecks and a nutty aroma that plays off the delicate duck broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Kamo Ramen (鴨らーめん) — a clean, golden shoyu ramen built purely from duck, scallion, and water; the step-up Special version adds extra duck toppings and a duck wonton.
ramen.chashu: Duck breast confit (kamo mune confit) — poached low and slow until silky and tender, offering a delicate, gamey richness in place of pork chashu.
notable_toppings: Seasonal/selectable scallion varieties (the shop's namesake ingredient)

gf.note: Standard wheat noodles and a soy-sauce-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or sauce substitution is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built entirely on duck bones and meat with duck confit as the topping — no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Tokyo take on single-protein ramen: the broth is simmered for two full days from nothing but duck, scallion, and water, yielding a clear, golden, delicately savory soup rather than the cloudy richness of tonkotsu or the smoky punch of niboshi. It's part of a recent wave of Tokyo duck-ramen specialists prizing purity and quiet umami over heavy fat or bold aromatics.

name: 百日紅（さるすべり） (Menya Sarusuberi (Hyakujikkou))

ramen.chashu: Low-temperature-cooked Hokkaido pork shoulder loin, diced into cubes and mounded into the broth; a low-temp chicken chashu also appears among the toppings.

gf.note: Noodles are wheat, sourced from an outside noodle maker, and tare bases include shoyu; no gluten-free or alternative-noodle option is documented.

noodles.notes: Chewy (mochi-mochi), high-hydration thick straight noodles supplied by an outside noodle factory, made to hold up to the concentrated dipping broth; a medium-thin straight noodle is used for some of the soup-ramen bowls.

ramen.pantheon: This bowl fuses two of Tokyo's favorite tsukemen currents into one cup — a punchy niboshi (dried-sardine) fish stock married to a pork-bone tonkotsu base — poured thick and concentrated over springy, high-hydration noodles built to carry it. Alongside the signature dipping bowl sits a lighter, clear shio line and a cheap, well-loved morning ramen, giving the shop range from big and briny to clean and gentle.
ramen.signature_bowl: 濃厚煮干しつけ麺 (Rich Niboshi Tsukemen) — a niboshi-and-tonkotsu blended dipping broth served with thick, chewy noodles

vegan.note: Broth is built on niboshi (dried sardine) fish stock and pork-bone tonkotsu, with pork and chicken chashu throughout the menu; no plant-based broth is documented.

name: 後楽園飯店 (Korakuen Hanten)

gf.note: Wheat noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) based broth — no gluten-free option is documented.

noodles.notes: Diner accounts disagree on whether the noodle is thin or on the thicker side, so thickness/shape could not be confirmed from available sources.

notable_toppings: Whole braised shark fin (fukahire no sugatani) set atop the bowl
ramen.pantheon: This isn't a regional ramen style but a one-of-a-kind institution born at the ballpark's doorstep: legend has it that Yomiuri Giants great Shigeo Nagashima, a fan of both noodles and shark fin, asked the kitchen to combine them, and the shop's signature bowl was born. A soy-based broth built on chicken stock, kombu, and dried scallop carries deep, clean umami, crowned with a whole braised shark fin ladled in intact rather than shredded — a luxury spin on old-school Tokyo chuka soba.
ramen.signature_bowl: Shop specialty Shark Fin Noodle Soup (飯店名物 フカヒレの姿煮入り汁そば / "Fukahire Ramen") — a whole braised shark fin served over soy-based noodle soup, roughly ¥4,600.

vegan.note: Signature bowl is built on shark fin and chicken-based broth — no plant-based option is documented.

name: 精進ラーメン菜 (Shojin Ramen Sai)

gf.note: No gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented; noodles appear to be standard wheat ramen noodles and the shoyu tare line likely contains wheat, so this is not celiac-safe.

noodles.notes: No noodle specifications (thickness, hydration, handmade status) were found in available sources.

ramen.pantheon: A newcomer to Shibuya's back streets built entirely around shojin ryori, the Buddhist temple-cuisine tradition of cooking without meat, fish, or dairy — its signature yuzu-shio bowl draws its whole soul from a pure vegetable dashi brightened with domestic yuzu, deliberately skipping even the soy-based mock meats other vegan ramen shops lean on. A richer, cloudier tonkotsu-style vegetable broth and a shoyu bowl round out the vegan-only menu for those who want more weight in the cup.
ramen.signature_bowl: Vegan Yuzu Shio Ramen — a clear, plant-based vegetable dashi seasoned with salt and brightened with domestic yuzu citrus

vegan.note: Entirely vegan / shojin ryori concept shop: every broth and ingredient is plant-based with no meat, fish, dairy, or animal-derived stock, and the shop notably avoids soy-based mock-meat substitutes as well.

name: 中国菜館 志苑 (Chugoku Saikan Shien)

noodles.notes: Noodle style not documented; this is a Chinese-cuisine restaurant rather than a noodle specialist, so noodle sourcing/cut is unconfirmed.

ramen.signature_bowl: 特製担々麺 (Special Tantanmen) — the shop's most-ordered dish, finished with a fragrant sakura-shrimp oil; a soup-less (汁なし) version is also offered.

gf.note: No gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented; standard wheat noodles and a soy/doubanjiang-based tare are used.

vegan.note: The signature tantanmen is built on spicy minced pork; no plant-based broth or topping swap is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A Sichuan-leaning Chinese restaurant rather than a dedicated ramen shop, its claim to noodle fame is a tantanmen built on spicy minced pork and crisp bean sprouts, warmed with a whisper of sansho rather than a heavy numbing hit. What sets the bowl apart is a fragrant sakura-shrimp oil finished over the top, giving it a briny, toasty aroma that's unusual among Tokyo's tantanmen.

name: らぁめん 満来 (Raamen Manrai)

ramen.chashu: Thick-cut shoulder-loin pork (kata-rosu), braised until it falls apart tender — sliced over a centimeter thick and piled seven-plus pieces high, so the meat itself is the whole point of the bowl.

gf.note: Wheat-based curly noodles and a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare alternative is documented.

noodles.notes: Curly, yellow kansui noodles with a plump, chewy bite — described by regulars as notably firm-textured; portions run generous even at the standard size.

notable_toppings: natto (on the shop's well-known natto-zaru variant)
ramen.pantheon: A holdover from Showa-era Tokyo shoyu ramen: a gently simmered, amber-clear pork-and-chicken bone broth seasoned with soy sauce, ladled over chewy curly noodles and all but buried under a mountain of thick, fall-apart shoulder-loin chashu. The real cult order is the cold "zaru" tsukemen version, piled with seven-plus inch-thick pork slices you have to dig through before you reach the noodles.
ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu Zaru (チャーシューざる) — cold dipping noodles heaped with a mountain of thick-cut chashu; the hot ramen version with the same chashu pile is the other classic order.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork and chicken bones, and the bowl is defined by its pork chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

name: ジカセイ メンショウ (Jikasei MENSHO (Shibuya PARCO))

ramen.chashu: No animal chashu on the vegan bowls — miso-and-spice marinated plant "soy meat" stands in for it; non-vegan diners can instead add A5 wagyu roast beef or Japanese pork chashu as a topping.

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-based (blended with quinoa to cut sugar/gluten content, not to eliminate it), so not celiac-safe — ask staff before ordering.

noodles.notes: House-made daily, wheat noodles blended with quinoa for a lighter, lower-gluten chew.

ramen.pantheon: This Shibuya PARCO offshoot of the globe-trotting MENSHO group builds its soul around plant milk: a soy-milk-and-sesame broth spiked with housemade chili oil and studded with miso-marinated soy "meat" proves a fully animal-free bowl can still taste as creamy and umami-rich as a classic tantanmen. It shares the kitchen with the shop's A5 wagyu chicken-paitan bowls, so vegans and meat-eaters can order side by side from the same counter.
ramen.signature_bowl: Vegan Tantanmen — a creamy soy-milk-and-sesame broth finished with housemade chili oil, topped with miso-marinated plant "soy meat," peanuts, and vegetables over housemade quinoa-blend noodles.

vegan.note: Dedicated vegan menu confirmed plant-based throughout: Vegan Tantanmen, Vegan Dry (broth-less) Tantanmen, Vegan Miso Ramen, and Spicy Vegan Miso Ramen — soy-milk/sesame or mushroom-based broths with soy-meat toppings, no fish dashi or animal fat.

name: 油そば総本店 新宿東口アルタ裏店 (Aburasoba Sohonten Shinjuku Higashiguchi Alt)

ramen.chashu: Roughly hand-chopped braised pork chashu (zaku-giri style, cut into chunky pieces rather than sliced), tossed through the noodles rather than laid on top.

gf.note: Wheat noodles and a wheat-based shoyu tare are used throughout; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

noodles.notes: Chewy (mochi-mochi), medium-thick wheat noodles supplied by the chain's own noodle-making operation; served dry (no broth) and tossed hot with tare and fragrant oil, finished tableside with vinegar and rayu chili oil to taste.

ramen.pantheon: This Alta-ura shop belongs to Tokyo's abura-soba (oil noodle) lineage, a soupless mazesoba style traced to early-1950s Musashino/Kunitachi noodle shops: there's no broth at all, just chewy medium-thick wheat noodles tossed hot with a concentrated shoyu tare and fragrant blended oil, then finished tableside with vinegar and chili rayu so every bite is coated, not swimming.
ramen.signature_bowl: Classic abura-soba: chewy medium-thick noodles tossed with rich shoyu tare and blended fragrant oil, topped with chunky chashu, extra-thick menma, scallion, mizuna and nori, then mixed tableside with vinegar and rayu chili oil to taste.

vegan.note: No plant-based tare, oil, or chashu-free bowl is documented; pork chashu and animal-derived tare/oil are standard.

name: 中華そば 勝本 (Chuka Soba Katsumoto)

ramen.chashu: Shoulder loin (kata-rosu) pork chashu, braised until moist and tender — a soft, delicate slice chosen to match the refined broth rather than a fatty, heavy cut.

gf.note: Noodles are wheat from a specialty noodle manufacturer and the tare is soy-based (shoyu); no gluten-free option is documented.

noodles.notes: Custom noodles from the noted Tokyo noodle manufactory Asakusa Kaikaro; springy, medium noodles chosen to carry the broth well.

ramen.pantheon: A jewel-clear niboshi-shoyu chuka soba where classical French kitchen discipline meets old-school Tokyo ramen: a soy broth built on dried sardines from Kyushu and chicken, finished with a pool of golden chicken fat, so the soup reads delicate and dashi-forward rather than the bolder, murkier niboshi bowls found elsewhere in Japan.
ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei Chuka Soba (special chuka soba) — the fully dressed bowl with chashu, ajitama, menma, and nori atop the niboshi-chicken shoyu broth.

vegan.note: Broth is built on dried sardines and chicken with pork chashu; no plant-based bowl is documented.

name: 鯛担麺専門店 恋し鯛 (Koishitai (Tai-Tantanmen Specialty Shop))

ramen.chashu: Low-temperature-cooked pork loin chashu served rare (blush-pink center, French sous-vide technique) alongside a soft, moist chicken confit — a French-kitchen take rather than a traditional simmered slab.

gf.note: Wheat-based curly noodles and a sesame/soy-leaning tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare swap is documented.

noodles.notes: Custom-developed curly (chijire) noodles built to cling to the thick sesame-tai broth; exact thickness shifts by menu item (some versions run thicker), and hydration is not documented.

ramen.pantheon: A French-trained chef's love letter to ramen: Uwajima sea bream is simmered into a thick, creamy paitan and married with chicken white broth and a house sesame sauce, then finished tantanmen-style with a drizzle of balsamic vinegar for a savory-sweet top note you won't find anywhere else in the genre.
ramen.signature_bowl: 鯛担麺 (Tai Tantanmen) — Uwajima sea-bream and chicken paitan thickened with an original sesame sauce, topped with rare pork chashu, chicken confit, and spicy meat sauce, finished with a balsamic vinegar drizzle; spice level adjustable to taste.

vegan.note: Broth is built on sea bream and chicken stock with meat-based chashu and confit throughout; no plant-based bowl is documented.

name: らーめん 穀雨 (Ramen Kokū)

ramen.chashu: Roasted, not braised: two cuts served together — leg (momo) hung and roasted plain, and shoulder-loin (kata-rosu) coated in honey and oven-roasted — giving a drier, more fragrant yakibuta character than the usual simmered chashu.

gf.note: Wheat noodles from an outside noodle maker, wheat-based shoyu tare, and wheat wonton wrappers — no gluten-free option is documented.

noodles.notes: Custom order noodles from a Tokyo noodle maker (Taiei Shokuhin): medium-thin, wavy, medium hydration, with a firm bite yet smooth, supple throat-feel.

notable_toppings: housemade-style ginger-scented pork wontons (3 or 5 per bowl)
optional table-side crispy fried scallion (age-negi) and housemade garlic chili oil
ramen.pantheon: This bowl carries the old-school Tokyo wonton-noodle bloodline of the legendary Tantan-tei, passed down through Meguro's Kazu-ya — a clear pork-and-chicken stock laced with dried bonito, kelp and shiitake that tastes light on first sip but unfolds into a deep, drinkable koku. It's classic shitamachi-style chuka soba, built around silky hand-folded wontons and honey-roasted (not braised) chashu rather than any modern richness arms race.
ramen.signature_bowl: Shoyu Wantan-men — clear pork-chicken-seafood broth with plump ginger-scented wontons (choice of 3 or 5) and two styles of honey-roasted chashu; the shio version is equally well-regarded.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork and chicken bones with bonito/kombu/shiitake dashi, and every noodle dish comes with meat-based chashu and/or pork wontons — no plant-based bowl is documented.

name: 中華菜房 達磨 (Chuka Saibo Daruma)

gf.note: Standard wheat kansui noodles and shoyu/shio tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

noodles.notes: Described as firm thin noodles in the spicy shoyu ramen; the shop's tanmen instead uses curled medium noodles (~150g). No evidence noodles are handmade in-house — likely a standard delivered noodle typical of town Chinese restaurants.

ramen.pantheon: A forty-plus-year Shinjuku machi-chuka standby that still cooks ramen the old Tokyo way: light, clear chicken-and-vegetable broth, wok-charred stir-fried vegetables piled over curly noodles for the tanmen, and a shoyu ramen spiked with chili that carries the shop's own name. It is comfort-food Tokyo shoyu rather than a boutique bowl — the kind of everyday ramen a neighborhood has relied on for decades.
ramen.signature_bowl: 達磨辛子ラーメン (Daruma Spicy Ramen) — a spicy shoyu ramen that looks fierier than it tastes, built on a flavorful clear broth with firm thin noodles; the shop's shio-based tanmen (loaded with over five stir-fried vegetables and meat) is an equally popular order.

vegan.note: Broths are chicken/animal-based and toppings include pork; no plant-based bowl is documented.
